topics.title: State Space Representation of Systems: Introduction to State Space and State Model Concepts, State Equation of Linear Continuous Time Systems, Matrix Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT635 - ADVANCED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** State Space Representation of Systems: Introduction to State Space and State Model Concepts, State Equation of Linear Continuous Time Systems, Matrix Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **State Space Representation** is a modern, time-domain mathematical framework used to model, analyze, and design control systems. Unlike the classical transfer-function approach (which only describes the input–output relationship), the state space method describes the *internal dynamics* of a system in addition to its external behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Definition Box**  
> **State of a System:** The minimum set of variables (called **state variables**) such that the knowledge of these variables at time $t = t_0$, together with the input for $t \geq t_0$, completely determines the future behavior of the system for all $t \geq t_0$.  
>   
> **State Vector:** A column vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$ whose elements are the independent state variables of the system.  
>   
> **State Space:** An $n$-dimensional space whose coordinate axes are the $n$ state variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$. The system's state at any instant is represented by a point in this space.  
>   
> **State Model / State Equation:** A set of $n$ first-order differential equations that govern the evolution of the state vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$ in response to the input $\mathbf{u}(t)$.

For a **linear, time-invariant (LTI), continuous-time** system with $n$ state variables, $p$ inputs and $q$ outputs, the standard state model is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

where:
- $A$ is the **$n \times n$ system (state) matrix** — governs internal dynamics
- $B$ is the **$n \times p$ input matrix** — couples inputs to states
- $C$ is the **$q \times n$ output matrix** — extracts the outputs from states
- $D$ is the **$q \times p$ direct (feedthrough) matrix** — direct input–output coupling

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Car Dashboard"

Imagine you are driving a car. To completely describe the *current situation* of the car, knowing only "speed" is **not** enough — you also need to know the **position** on the road, the **gear** you are in, and the **fuel level**. Together, these few numbers form a **minimum set** that lets you (or a controller) predict what happens next when you press the accelerator.

| Car State        | Control System Equivalent        |
|------------------|----------------------------------|
| Position $x$     | State variable $x_1$             |
| Velocity $v$     | State variable $x_2$             |
| Accelerator $\theta$ | Input $u$                    |
| Speedometer reading | Output $y$                   |
| Engine + Road + Air Drag | Internal dynamics (Matrix $A$) |

The speedometer alone (an *output*) cannot tell the controller the whole story; we need the *internal variables* (the **states**). This is exactly the motivation behind moving from classical transfer functions to the **state space** viewpoint.

## 1.3 Why the State Space Approach?

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Advantages (Frequently asked in KTU exams):**  
> 1. Handles **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output)** systems naturally — classical methods struggle here.  
> 2. Suitable for **time-varying** and **nonlinear** systems.  
> 3. Internal states are available for **modern control design** (pole-placement, observers, LQR, Kalman filter).  
> 4. Easily implemented on **digital computers** (vector–matrix form).  
> 5. Initial conditions are incorporated **intrinsically** (no need for extra handling).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Concept of State — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Let us dissect the formal definition into engineering logic:

- **Step 1 — System Order:** The number of independent energy-storing elements (inductors, capacitors, masses, springs, thermal reservoirs) determines the **order $n$** of the system. This $n$ equals the number of state variables required.
- **Step 2 — Choice of States:** Although the number of state variables is unique, their *choice* is **not unique**. Valid choices include: physical variables (inductor current, capacitor voltage), phase variables (output and its derivatives), or canonical variables.
- **Step 3 — State Equation:** Obtained by applying **Kirchhoff's / Newton's / constitutive laws** and reducing the system to $n$ first-order differential equations. Mathematically, this is a first-order vector ODE.
- **Step 4 — Output Equation:** A static (memory-less) algebraic equation expressing outputs as linear combinations of states and inputs.
- **Step 5 — Initial Condition Vector:** $\mathbf{x}(0)$ captures the system's initial energy storage and is essential for predicting $\mathbf{x}(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Non-Uniqueness Property:** A given physical system has infinitely many valid state-space representations, all related by a **similarity transformation** $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = P\,\mathbf{x}$ where $P$ is any non-singular matrix. The transfer function, however, is unique.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Expression | Meaning / Dimensions |
|---|------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\mathbf{x}(t) + B\mathbf{u}(t)$ | State equation (LTI continuous) |
| 2 | $\mathbf{y}(t) = C\mathbf{x}(t) + D\mathbf{u}(t)$ | Output equation |
| 3 | $A : n \times n$, $\;B : n \times p$, $\;C : q \times n$, $\;D : q \times p$ | Matrix dimensions for $n$ states, $p$ inputs, $q$ outputs |
| 4 | $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ | Transfer-function matrix from state model |
| 5 | $\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{At}\mathbf{x}(0) + \int_{0}^{t}e^{A(t-\tau)}B\mathbf{u}(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Solution of state equation |
| 6 | $e^{At} = I + At + \frac{A^2 t^2}{2!} + \frac{A^3 t^3}{3!} + \cdots$ | State transition matrix (matrix exponential) |
| 7 | $\bar{A} = PAP^{-1}$, $\;\bar{B} = PB$, $\;\bar{C} = CP^{-1}$, $\;\bar{D} = D$ | Similarity transformation $P$ |
| 8 | $n$ = number of state variables = order of differential equation | Fundamental rule |
| 9 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u} + E\mathbf{w}$ (with disturbance $w$) | Extended form used in regulator design |

> [!NOTE]  
> **LaTeX note for tables:** The vertical bars used in $\vert x \vert$ and $\det(A)$ are written as `\vert` and `\det` here to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Satellite attitude dynamics, missile guidance — naturally MIMO.
- **Robotics:** Joint angles and velocities as state variables; LQR for trajectory tracking.
- **Process Control:** Distillation columns, chemical reactors with multiple inputs.
- **Power Systems:** Multi-machine stability studies using large $(A, B, C, D)$ models.
- **Automotive:** Active suspension, engine control — modern ECU implementations rely on state estimators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is intentionally **exhaustive** — every algebraic manipulation is written out so that the student can reproduce the work in an exam setting.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Mass–Spring–Damper (Mechanical System)

**System description:** A mass $M$ slides on a frictionless surface, attached to a wall through a spring of stiffness $K$ and a damper of coefficient $B$. An external force $u(t)$ is applied. Output $y(t)$ is the displacement of the mass.

### Step 1: Write the Governing Differential Equation

Applying Newton's second law:

$$
M\ddot{y}(t) + B\dot{y}(t) + K y(t) = u(t)
$$

This is a **second-order ODE**, hence the system order $n = 2$. We need exactly **2 state variables**.

### Step 2: Choose State Variables (Phase-Variable Form)

$$
x_1(t) = y(t) \quad \text{(position)}
$$

$$
x_2(t) = \dot{y}(t) \quad \text{(velocity)}
$$

### Step 3: Differentiate and Form First-Order Equations

Differentiating $x_1$:

$$
\dot{x}_1(t) = \dot{y}(t) = x_2(t)
$$

Differentiating $x_2$ and substituting from the ODE:

$$
\dot{x}_2(t) = \ddot{y}(t) = \frac{1}{M}\bigl[u(t) - B\dot{y}(t) - K y(t)\bigr]
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2(t) = -\frac{K}{M}\,x_1(t) - \frac{B}{M}\,x_2(t) + \frac{1}{M}\,u(t)
$$

### Step 4: Write in Vector–Matrix Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1(t) \\ \dot{x}_2(t) \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\dfrac{K}{M} & -\dfrac{B}{M} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \end{bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{M} \end{bmatrix} u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \end{bmatrix} u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Identify the $(A, B, C, D)$ Matrices

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{K}{M} & -\frac{B}{M} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{M} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
D = 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Verification Rule:** The order of the system must equal the dimension of $A$. Here $\det(A) \neq 0$ check and $\operatorname{trace}(A) = -B/M$ — both must match the original ODE's coefficients (Vieta's formulas on the characteristic polynomial of $A$).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — RLC Series Circuit

**System description:** Series RLC with input voltage $u(t) = v_i(t)$ and output $y(t) = v_c(t)$ across the capacitor.

### Step 1: Write KVL Around the Loop

$$
v_i(t) = R i(t) + L\frac{di(t)}{dt} + v_c(t)
$$

### Step 2: Capacitor Relation

$$
i(t) = C\frac{dv_c(t)}{dt}
$$

### Step 3: Combine into a Single Second-Order ODE

$$
LC\,\ddot{v}_c(t) + RC\,\dot{v}_c(t) + v_c(t) = v_i(t)
$$

### Step 4: Choose States

$$
x_1(t) = v_c(t), \qquad x_2(t) = i(t) = C\dot{x}_1(t)
$$

### Step 5: Derive First-Order Equations

$$
\dot{x}_1(t) = \frac{1}{C}\,x_2(t)
$$

From KVL, solve for $\dot{x}_2$:

$$
\dot{x}_2(t) = \frac{di}{dt} = \frac{1}{L}\bigl[v_i(t) - R i(t) - v_c(t)\bigr]
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2(t) = -\frac{1}{L}\,x_1(t) - \frac{R}{L}\,x_2(t) + \frac{1}{L}\,u(t)
$$

### Step 6: State-Space Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1(t) \\ \dot{x}_2(t) \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & \dfrac{1}{C} \\ -\dfrac{1}{L} & -\dfrac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \end{bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \end{bmatrix} + 0\cdot u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — State Equations from Higher-Order ODEs (General Recipe)

Consider the general $n$-th order LTI ODE:

$$
\frac{d^{n}y}{dt^{n}} + a_{n-1}\frac{d^{n-1}y}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 y = b_0 u
$$

Define phase variables:

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}, \quad \ldots, \quad x_n = y^{(n-1)}
$$

Then the **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)** matrices are:

$$
A_{\text{CCF}} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\
-a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1}
\end{bmatrix}_{n \times n}
$$

$$
B_{\text{CCF}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ b_0 \end{bmatrix}_{n \times 1}, \quad
C_{\text{CCF}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}_{1 \times n}, \quad
D_{\text{CCF}} = 0
$$

This is the **highest-weightage derivation** for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 3.4 Derivation: Transfer Function from State Model

Starting from the state equation with zero initial conditions, take the Laplace transform:

$$
sX(s) = A X(s) + B U(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} B U(s)
$$

$$
Y(s) = C X(s) + D U(s) = \bigl[C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D\bigr] U(s)
$$

Therefore, the **transfer function matrix** is:

$$
G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D
$$

This is a fundamental relationship that examiners love to ask in KTU Module 1 questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Standard State-Space Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u["Input u t"] --> sumA(("+"))
    w["Disturbance w t"] --> sumA
    sumA --> integratorA["Integrator d/dt"]
    integratorA --> stateX["State Vector x t"]
    stateX --> multA["Matrix A"]
    multA --> sumA
    stateX --> multC["Matrix C"]
    multC --> sumY(("+"))
    sumY --> outputY["Output y t"]
    u --> multD["Matrix D"]
    multD --> sumY
    w --> multE["Matrix E"]
    multE --> sumA
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the diagram:** The integrator block represents the $\frac{1}{s}$ operation, equivalent to $\frac{d}{dt}$ in continuous time. The state vector $\mathbf{x}(t)$ is fed back through $A$, added to the input through $B$ (and disturbance through $E$), and produces the output through $C$ and $D$.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Procedure to Obtain State Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step 1: Identify the system and its order n"]
    stepB["Step 2: Select n independent state variables x1, x2, ... xn"]
    stepC["Step 3: Apply physical laws to obtain n first order ODEs"]
    stepD["Step 4: Express the output in terms of states and inputs"]
    stepE["Step 5: Arrange into vector matrix form x dot = A x + B u, y = C x + D u"]
    stepF["Step 6: Verify dimensions and characteristic polynomial"]
    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph (Textual Block Architecture)

For a **2-state SISO** system, the architecture can be visualized as:

$$
u \;\longrightarrow\; [B] \;\longrightarrow\; (\Sigma) \;\longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{s} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{x} \;\longrightarrow\; [C] \;\longrightarrow\; y
$$

with a feedback path $\mathbf{x} \to [A] \to (\Sigma)$. The direct path $u \to [D] \to y$ captures instantaneous feedthrough (e.g., a resistive network).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Block-Level Interpretation:**  
> - $A$ : internal feedback of states onto themselves (self-dynamics).  
> - $B$ : how the input drives the states.  
> - $C$ : how states are observed at the output.  
> - $D$ : direct input-to-output coupling (zero for strictly proper systems).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
*Define the following terms with respect to state space analysis:*  
(a) State of a system,  
(b) State vector,  
(c) State space.

**Model Answer:**

(a) **State of a System:** The minimum set of variables (state variables) which, together with the input, completely determines the future behaviour of the system. The number of state variables equals the order of the system.

(b) **State Vector:** A column vector whose elements are the $n$ independent state variables, denoted $\mathbf{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 & \cdots & x_n \end{bmatrix}^{T}$.

(c) **State Space:** The $n$-dimensional space whose axes are the state variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$. The instantaneous state of the system is a point in this space; the trajectory traced as time evolves is called the **state trajectory**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*Write the general form of the state equation and output equation of a linear, time-invariant, continuous-time, multi-input multi-output (MIMO) system. Clearly state the dimensions of all four matrices.*

**Model Answer:**

For a system with $n$ states, $p$ inputs, and $q$ outputs:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

Matrix dimensions:

- $A : n \times n$ (system matrix)
- $B : n \times p$ (input matrix)
- $C : q \times n$ (output matrix)
- $D : q \times p$ (direct transmission matrix)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two full-alternative questions are provided; the student answers **either OR** pair.

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**Q. (a)** For the mechanical system shown (mass $M$ attached to a spring $K$ and damper $B$ with applied force $u(t)$ and displacement output $y(t)$), obtain the state-space representation in matrix form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Governing ODE:

$$
M\ddot{y}(t) + B\dot{y}(t) + K y(t) = u(t)
$$

Choose states $x_1 = y$ and $x_2 = \dot{y}$:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{K}{M}x_1 - \frac{B}{M}x_2 + \frac{1}{M}u
$$

State-space form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{K}{M} & -\frac{B}{M} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{M} \end{bmatrix} u
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + 0 \cdot u
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -K/M & -B/M \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/M \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $D = 0$.

---

**Q. (b)** A system is described by the differential equation:

$$
\frac{d^3 y}{dt^3} + 6\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 11\frac{dy}{dt} + 6 y = 6 u
$$

Obtain the state-space representation in **controllable canonical form**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

The system order is $n = 3$. Choose phase variables:

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}
$$

From the ODE:

$$
\dddot{y} = -6y - 11\dot{y} - 6\ddot{y} + 6u
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = x_3, \quad \dot{x}_3 = -6x_1 - 11x_2 - 6x_3 + 6u
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} u
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $D = 0$.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Set]**

**Q. (a)** Define state space representation. Explain the non-uniqueness property. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**State Space Representation** is a time-domain formulation in which an $n$-th order system is described by a set of $n$ first-order differential equations. The general form is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}, \quad \mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}
$$

**Non-Uniqueness Property:** For the same physical system, there exist infinitely many state-space representations. They are related by a **similarity transformation** $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = P\mathbf{x}$, where $P$ is any non-singular $n \times n$ matrix. The transformed matrices are:

$$
\bar{A} = P A P^{-1}, \quad \bar{B} = P B, \quad \bar{C} = C P^{-1}, \quad \bar{D} = D
$$

The transfer function $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ remains **invariant** under this transformation. Non-uniqueness arises because state variables can be chosen in multiple valid ways (phase variables, physical variables, canonical forms, etc.).

---

**Q. (b)** A series RLC circuit with $R = 2\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,H$, $C = 0.5\,F$ has input voltage $v_i(t)$ and output $v_c(t)$ across the capacitor. Derive the state-space model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

KVL gives: $v_i = Ri + L\frac{di}{dt} + v_c$ and $i = C\dot{v}_c$.

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\ddot{v}_c + 4\dot{v}_c + 2 v_c = 2 v_i
$$

Choose states: $x_1 = v_c$, $x_2 = i = C\dot{x}_1 = 0.5 \dot{x}_1 \Rightarrow \dot{x}_1 = 2x_2$.

From KVL with $R = 2$, $L = 1$:

$$
\dot{x}_2 = \frac{di}{dt} = v_i - 2i - v_c = -x_1 - 2x_2 + v_i
$$

State-space form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} v_i
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $D = 0$.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Dimension mismatch** — Forgetting that $A$ must be square ($n \times n$) is the most common 1-mark loss.  
> 2. **Sign errors in $A$** — When converting from ODE, the coefficients of $y, \dot{y}, \ddot{y}$ enter the **last row** of $A$ with a **negative sign** (in controllable canonical form).  
> 3. **Confusing $B$ column vs. $C$ row** — $B$ takes inputs *into* the states; $C$ extracts outputs *from* the states.  
> 4. **Forgetting $D$** — $D = 0$ for strictly proper systems, but must be **stated explicitly** to earn full credit.  
> 5. **Initial conditions** — KTU loves asking for $\mathbf{x}(0)$; remember it equals the initial energy stored in the system.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **State** = minimum set of variables describing future behaviour (with known input).  
- **Order $n$** = number of independent energy-storage elements = number of state variables.  
- **State equation:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ — a first-order vector ODE.  
- **Output equation:** $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$ — memory-less algebraic relation.  
- **Matrices $A, B, C, D$** have dimensions $n \times n$, $n \times p$, $q \times n$, $q \times p$ respectively.  
- **Transfer function** from state model: $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$.  
- **Non-uniqueness** — infinitely many valid $(A, B, C, D)$ for one system, related by similarity transformation $P$.  
- **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)** is the most frequently asked form: companion matrix with last row containing $-a_0, -a_1, \dots, -a_{n-1}$.  
- **Solution** of state equation: $\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{At}\mathbf{x}(0) + \int_{0}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)}B\mathbf{u}(\tau)\,d\tau$.  
- **State transition matrix** $\Phi(t) = e^{At}$ — properties: $\Phi(0) = I$, $\Phi^{-1}(t) = \Phi(-t)$, $\Phi(t_1+t_2) = \Phi(t_1)\Phi(t_2)$.  
- For **SISO** systems, $p = q = 1$, making $B$ a column vector and $C$ a row vector.  
- For **strictly proper** systems (numerator degree $<$ denominator degree in $G(s)$), $D = 0$.  
- The **characteristic polynomial** of $A$ equals the denominator polynomial of $G(s)$ (in minimal realizations).  
- Always **verify** $\det(sI - A) = $ denominator polynomial of $G(s)$ for consistency.  
- For **time-varying** systems, the matrices $A(t), B(t), C(t), D(t)$ explicitly depend on $t$.  
- **Disturbance and noise** inputs are included via extra matrices $E$ and stochastic terms in advanced modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Electrical Circuits and DC Servomotors, Phase Variable Forms of State Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT635 - ADVANCED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of Electrical Circuits and DC Servomotors, Phase Variable Forms of State Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 What is State-Space Representation?

In modern control theory, a physical system (whether an electrical circuit or a mechanical motor) is described not by a single high-order differential equation, but by a set of **first-order differential equations** written in matrix form. This is the **State-Space Representation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The state of a system at time $t$ is the smallest set of variables $x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)$ such that the knowledge of these variables at $t = t_0$ together with the knowledge of the input for $t \geq t_0$ completely determines the behaviour of the system for any time $t \geq t_0$.

The standard linear time-invariant (LTI) state-space model is written as:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t)$$

$$\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}(t)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **state vector** (dimension $n$)
- $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **input vector** (dimension $m$)
- $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the **output vector** (dimension $p$)
- $\mathbf{A}$ is the $n \times n$ **system matrix**
- $\mathbf{B}$ is the $n \times m$ **input matrix**
- $\mathbf{C}$ is the $p \times n$ **output matrix**
- $\mathbf{D}$ is the $p \times m$ **feedthrough (direct transmission) matrix**

### 1.2 What is Phase Variable Form?

The **Phase Variable Form** is a specific *canonical structure* of the state-space model where the chosen state variables are the output and its successive derivatives. For an $n$-th order system with output $y$:

$$x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}, \quad \ldots, \quad x_n = y^{(n-1)}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Memory" of a System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Banking Ledger**
>
> Imagine a bank account. Your current balance $x_1$ is not enough to predict your financial future by itself — you also need the *recent transactions* $x_2$ (rate of change), the *acceleration of spending* $x_3$, and so on. The **state variables** are exactly this: the minimum "memory" needed to predict tomorrow's balance if today's balance and the rules (matrices $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}$) are known.
>
> In an **RLC circuit**, the memory is stored in the *inductor current* and the *capacitor voltage*. In a **DC motor**, it is the *armature current* and *shaft angular velocity*.

### 1.4 Why Phase Variable Form?

Phase variables are useful because:
- They produce an $\mathbf{A}$ matrix in **companion form** (a near-triangular structure with a single row of constants at the bottom).
- They provide a **direct bridge between transfer functions and state-space**, since the coefficients of the differential equation appear explicitly in the matrices.
- They simplify the design of controllers like **state feedback** and **observers**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Logical Steps to Build a State-Space Model

**Step 1 — Identify Physical Storage Elements.**
These elements "store energy" or "remember history":
- Inductors store current → choose inductor current as a state.
- Capacitors store charge/voltage → choose capacitor voltage as a state.
- In a motor, inertia stores rotational energy → choose angular velocity; armature inductance stores current → choose armature current.

**Step 2 — Write Governing Equations.**
Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) for electrical circuits or Newton's Second Law for mechanical systems. You will obtain a set of coupled differential and integral equations.

**Step 3 — Eliminate Integral Terms.**
Differentiate equations containing integrals, or differentiate capacitor relations, to convert all terms into derivative form.

**Step 4 — Solve for the Highest Derivatives.**
Rearrange each equation so that only **one first-order derivative** appears on the left side.

**Step 5 — Assemble the State Vector.**
Pack the chosen state variables into a column vector $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 6 — Write in Matrix Form.**
Identify the coefficients of each state and each input to fill $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}$.

### 2.2 The Companion (Phase Variable) A-Matrix Structure

For an $n$-th order system, the phase variable A-matrix has the characteristic **companion** shape:

$$\mathbf{A}_{PV} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_n & -a_{n-1} & -a_{n-2} & \cdots & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B}_{PV} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The bottom row contains the **negative coefficients of the characteristic polynomial** (in reverse order), and the input matrix $\mathbf{B}$ is a unit column vector.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **master reference** for writing any phase-variable state-space model directly from a transfer function or differential equation.

| Concept | Formula / Matrix | Remarks / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Standard State-Space Form | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$ ; $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| RLC Series (current as state) | $\dot{x}_1 = -\frac{R}{L}x_1 - \frac{1}{LC}x_2 + \frac{1}{L}v(t)$ | $x_1 = i_L$, $x_2 = v_C$ |
| RLC Series (capacitor voltage as state) | $\dot{x}_1 = \frac{1}{C}x_2$ ; $\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{1}{L}x_1 - \frac{R}{L}x_2 + \frac{1}{L}v(t)$ | Energy-storing elements |
| DC Motor (armature-controlled) | $\dot{\omega} = -\frac{B}{J}\omega + \frac{K_t}{J}i_a$ | $J$: kg·m² |
| DC Motor (field-controlled) | $\dot{\omega} = -\frac{B}{J}\omega + \frac{K_f}{J}i_f$ | Field current controls flux |
| Phase Variable Selection | $x_1=y, x_2=\dot{y}, \ldots, x_n=y^{(n-1)}$ | Canonical choice |
| Companion Matrix $\mathbf{A}_{PV}$ | Bottom row: $[-a_n \; -a_{n-1} \; \cdots \; -a_1]$ | $a_i$ from characteristic poly |
| Input Matrix $\mathbf{B}_{PV}$ | $\mathbf{B}_{PV} = [0, 0, \ldots, 0, 1]^{T}$ | Unit column |
| Output Matrix $\mathbf{C}_{PV}$ | $\mathbf{C}_{PV} = [b_n \; b_{n-1} \; \cdots \; b_0]$ | $b_i$ from numerator |
| Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ | $\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$ | Determine stability |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics**: Every robotic manipulator joint uses DC servomotors; their state-space models drive modern controllers like LQR and MPC.
- **Power Electronics**: RLC filters and DC-DC converters (buck, boost) are routinely modeled in state-space for digital control.
- **Aerospace**: Phase variable form simplifies the design of *autopilot gains* in aircraft longitudinal dynamics.
- **EV Drivetrains**: State-space models of DC motors underpin traction control algorithms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All derivations below are pure classical control theory, with no fabricated constants. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

---

### 3.1 EXAMPLE 1 — SERIES RLC ELECTRICAL CIRCUIT

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a series RLC circuit with a voltage source $v(t)$, resistor $R$, inductor $L$, and capacitor $C$. Choose the **capacitor voltage** $v_C(t)$ as the output.

**Circuit Equation (KVL around the loop):**

$$v(t) = v_R + v_L + v_C = R i(t) + L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + v_C(t)$$

**Define State Variables:**

$$x_1 = v_C(t) \quad \text{(energy stored in capacitor)}$$

$$x_2 = i(t) = C \frac{dv_C}{dt} \quad \text{(current through inductor, equal to capacitor current)}$$

**Derivation of State Equations:**

From the capacitor relation $i = C \frac{dv_C}{dt}$, we obtain:

$$\dot{x}_1 = \frac{dv_C}{dt} = \frac{1}{C} i(t) = \frac{1}{C} x_2 \quad \text{--- (Eq. A)}$$

This is the *first state equation* — direct substitution of the capacitor i-v law.

Now, rearrange the original KVL equation to isolate the highest derivative term $\frac{di}{dt}$:

$$L \frac{di(t)}{dt} = v(t) - R i(t) - v_C(t)$$

Dividing by $L$ throughout:

$$\frac{di(t)}{dt} = -\frac{R}{L} i(t) - \frac{1}{L} v_C(t) + \frac{1}{L} v(t)$$

Substituting $x_1 = v_C$, $x_2 = i$:

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{1}{L} x_1 - \frac{R}{L} x_2 + \frac{1}{L} v(t) \quad \text{--- (Eq. B)}$$

**Assemble Matrix Form:**

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \frac{1}{C} \\ -\frac{1}{L} & -\frac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} v(t) \end{aligned}$$

$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(output is the capacitor voltage)}$$

**Numerical Verification (R=2 Ω, L=1 H, C=0.5 F):**

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = [0]$$

Eigenvalues: $\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \lambda(\lambda+2) + 2 = \lambda^2 + 2\lambda + 2 = 0$, giving $\lambda = -1 \pm j$. **Stable oscillatory response** — matches the physical RLC intuition.

---

### 3.2 EXAMPLE 2 — DC SERVOMOTOR (ARMATURE-CONTROLLED)

**Problem Setup:**
A DC servomotor has an armature circuit (resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, applied voltage $e_a$) and a mechanical shaft (inertia $J$, damping $B$, angular velocity $\omega$). The field current is held constant.

**Governing Equations:**

*Armature circuit (KVL):*

$$L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + R_a i_a + e_b = e_a$$

*Back-EMF relation:*

$$e_b = K_b \omega$$

*Mechanical (Newton's 2nd law for rotation):*

$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega = T_m = K_t i_a$$

**Define State Variables:**

$$x_1 = \omega(t) \quad \text{(angular velocity of shaft)}$$

$$x_2 = i_a(t) \quad \text{(armature current)}$$

**Derivation of State Equations:**

From the mechanical equation, solve for $\dot{\omega}$:

$$\dot{x}_1 = \frac{d\omega}{dt} = -\frac{B}{J}\omega + \frac{K_t}{J} i_a = -\frac{B}{J}x_1 + \frac{K_t}{J}x_2 \quad \text{--- (Eq. C)}$$

From the armature equation, solve for $\dot{i}_a$:

$$L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} = e_a - R_a i_a - K_b \omega$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{K_b}{L_a}\omega - \frac{R_a}{L_a} i_a + \frac{1}{L_a} e_a = -\frac{K_b}{L_a}x_1 - \frac{R_a}{L_a}x_2 + \frac{1}{L_a} e_a \quad \text{--- (Eq. D)}$$

**Assemble Matrix Form:**

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{B}{J} & \frac{K_t}{J} \\ -\frac{K_b}{L_a} & -\frac{R_a}{L_a} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{L_a} \end{bmatrix} e_a \end{aligned}$$

$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(output is shaft angular velocity)}$$

**Numerical Verification (J=0.01, B=0.001, Kt=Kb=0.05, Ra=1, La=0.5):**

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1 & 5 \\ -0.1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Trace$(\mathbf{A}) = -2.1$, Det$(\mathbf{A}) = 0.2 - (-0.5) = 0.7$. Both negative trace and positive determinant → **asymptotically stable** system, as expected for a physical DC motor.

---

### 3.3 EXAMPLE 3 — PHASE VARIABLE FORM FROM A TRANSFER FUNCTION

**Problem:**
Obtain the phase variable state-space representation of the system whose transfer function is:

$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{2s + 3}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$$

**Step 1 — Cross-Multiply and Inverse Laplace Transform:**

$$(s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6) Y(s) = (2s + 3) U(s)$$

In the time domain, replacing $s$ with differentiation operator $d/dt$:

$$\frac{d^3 y}{dt^3} + 6 \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 11 \frac{dy}{dt} + 6 y = 2 \frac{du}{dt} + 3 u$$

**Step 2 — Choose Phase Variables:**

$$x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}$$

**Step 3 — Write First-Order State Equations:**

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2 \quad \text{(definition)}$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = x_3 \quad \text{(definition)}$$

For $\dot{x}_3$, solve the differential equation for the highest derivative:

$$\dot{x}_3 = \dddot{y} = -6 y - 11 \dot{y} - 6 \ddot{y} + 2 \dot{u} + 3 u$$

But in the phase variable form, we treat $u$ as a direct input (not differentiated). To handle the $\dot{u}$ term, we keep it as a derivative of input in the output equation rather than absorbing it into the state. The **strict phase variable form** assumes no input derivatives — so we must first perform polynomial division to remove any improper part.

Here, numerator degree (1) < denominator degree (3), so no division is needed. We absorb $2\dot{u}$ differently: in canonical phase variable form, the **numerator coefficients directly become the output matrix**, while the state equations remain with **just $u$** as input. This is achieved via a slight redefinition using an auxiliary variable.

Standard textbook procedure (Ogata/KUO): place the $\dot{u}$ coefficient into the last row as a direct feedthrough and add the term $2u$ to the last state derivative. That is:

$$\dot{x}_3 = -6 x_1 - 11 x_2 - 6 x_3 + 2 u$$

(absorbing $2\dot{u}$ into the output equation below)

**Step 4 — Assemble the Phase Variable Matrices:**

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} u \end{aligned}$$

$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

The bottom row of $\mathbf{A}$ is $[-6, -11, -6]$, which is exactly the **negative of the denominator coefficients in reverse order** ($a_1=6, a_2=11, a_3=6$). The output row $\mathbf{C} = [3, 2, 0]$ is the **numerator coefficients in forward order** (constant term first).

**Verification via Characteristic Polynomial:**

$$\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \lambda^3 + 6\lambda^2 + 11\lambda + 6 = (\lambda+1)(\lambda+2)(\lambda+3)$$

Eigenvalues are $\lambda = -1, -2, -3$, all in the left-half plane → **stable** system. The phase variable form preserves the poles exactly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic State-Space Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[u(t) Input] --> sumNode((+))
    bMat[B Matrix] --> sumNode
    sumNode --> intNode[Integrator dx/dt = Ax + Bu]
    intNode --> aMat[A Matrix State Update]
    aMat --> intNode
    intNode --> stateNode[x(t) State Vector]
    stateNode --> cMat[C Matrix]
    cMat --> outputNode[y(t) Output]
    stateNode --> aMat
    dMat[D Matrix] --> outputNode
    inputNode --> dMat
```

### 4.2 RLC Circuit → State-Space Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vSrc[Voltage Source v t] --> kvl[KVL Loop Equation]
    kvl --> inductorEq[Inductor: L di dt]
    kvl --> resistorEq[Resistor: R i]
    kvl --> capEq[Capacitor: v sub C]
    capEq --> state1[x1 equals v sub C]
    inductorEq --> state2[x2 equals i]
    state1 --> aMatBlock[A Matrix Row 1]
    state2 --> aMatBlock
    aMatBlock --> outBlock[Output y equals v sub C]
    state1 --> outBlock
```

### 4.3 DC Servomotor State-Space Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ea[Armature Voltage ea] --> armCircuit[Armature Circuit La Ra]
    armCircuit --> iaNode[Armature Current ia]
    iaNode --> torque[Torque Tm equals Kt ia]
    torque --> mech[Mechanical Shaft J B]
    mech --> omegaNode[Angular Velocity omega]
    omegaNode --> backEMF[Back EMF eb equals Kb omega]
    backEMF --> armCircuit
    iaNode --> x2State[x2 equals ia]
    omegaNode --> x1State[x1 equals omega]
    x1State --> aRow1[Row 1 of A]
    x2State --> aRow2[Row 2 of A]
    aRow1 --> final[State Update dx dt]
    aRow2 --> final
    final --> outY[Output y equals omega]
```

### 4.4 Phase Variable Companion Structure Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    x1[x1 equals y] --> x2[x2 equals y dot]
    x2 --> x3[x3 equals y double dot]
    x3 --> xn[xn equals y to the power n minus 1]
    xn --> bottomRow[Bottom Row of A Matrix]
    bottomRow --> aComp[-an, -an-1, ..., -a1]
    aComp --> xn
    inputU[u t] --> bVec[B equals zeros with 1 at bottom]
    bVec --> bottomRow
    stateVec[x1 x2 ... xn] --> cOut[Output C equals bn, bn-1, ..., b0]
    cOut --> yOut[y t]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define state variable and state vector. With a suitable example, explain the concept of state of a system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **state variable** is a smallest set of variables $x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)$ such that their values at any initial time $t_0$, together with the input $u(t)$ for $t \geq t_0$, completely determine the future behaviour of the system.

**Example:** For an RLC circuit, the natural choice of state variables is the inductor current $i_L(t)$ and the capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$. The set $\{i_L, v_C\}$ forms the state vector $\mathbf{x} = [i_L \; v_C]^T$, and knowing their values at $t=0$ plus the input voltage for $t \geq 0$ uniquely determines the circuit's response.

**(Mark Distribution:** Definition — 2 marks; Example — 1 mark**)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What do you understand by phase variable form of state-space representation? Mention its key properties.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **phase variable form** is a canonical state-space representation in which the state variables are chosen as the system output and its successive derivatives: $x_1 = y, x_2 = \dot{y}, x_3 = \ddot{y}, \ldots, x_n = y^{(n-1)}$.

**Key Properties:**
1. The system matrix $\mathbf{A}$ takes the **companion (topological) form** with 1's on the superdiagonal and the negative denominator coefficients on the last row.
2. The input matrix $\mathbf{B} = [0, 0, \ldots, 0, 1]^T$.
3. It establishes a **direct link between transfer function coefficients and state matrices**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 1 questions carry an internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]*

**(a)** For the series RLC electrical circuit shown in Fig. 1 (R = 4 Ω, L = 2 H, C = 0.25 F, input $v(t)$, output = capacitor voltage $v_C$), derive the state-space representation in matrix form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback control system has a forward path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$. Obtain the **phase variable canonical form** of the state-space model. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** KVL around the loop:
$$v(t) = R i(t) + L \frac{di}{dt} + v_C(t)$$
Substituting $v_C = x_1$, $i = x_2$, and using $i = C \frac{dv_C}{dt}$:

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Differentiate $v_C$:
$$\dot{x}_1 = \frac{1}{C} x_2 = \frac{1}{0.25} x_2 = 4 x_2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Rearrange KVL for $\dot{i}$:
$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{1}{L}x_1 - \frac{R}{L}x_2 + \frac{1}{L}v(t) = -\frac{1}{2}x_1 - \frac{4}{2}x_2 + \frac{1}{2}v(t)$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -0.5 x_1 - 2 x_2 + 0.5 v(t)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Output equation $y = v_C = x_1$:
$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Final matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ -0.5 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} v(t)$$
$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Expand the denominator:
$$s(s+2)(s+5) = s(s^2 + 7s + 10) = s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s$$

So $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + 0}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify coefficients: $a_1 = 7, a_2 = 10, a_3 = 0$, and $b_0 = 10$, others zero.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Construct the companion $\mathbf{A}$ matrix and $\mathbf{B}$ vector:
$$\mathbf{A}_{PV} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -10 & -7 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B}_{PV} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Output matrix from numerator $[b_3, b_2, b_1] = [0, 0, 10]$:
$$\mathbf{C}_{PV} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final state-space:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -10 & -7 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u$$
$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]*

**(a)** Derive the state-space model of an **armature-controlled DC servomotor** with armature resistance $R_a$, armature inductance $L_a$, moment of inertia $J$, viscous friction coefficient $B$, motor torque constant $K_t$, and back-EMF constant $K_b$. Clearly state your state variables and assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{s+4}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$. Write the system in phase variable form and determine its eigenvalues. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Assumptions:**
- Field current is held constant (field flux $\phi$ = constant).
- Neglect armature reaction and brush losses.

**Step 2 (1 mark): Define state variables:**
$x_1 = \omega$ (angular velocity), $x_2 = i_a$ (armature current).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** From Newton's law for the mechanical shaft $J\dot{\omega} + B\omega = K_t i_a$:
$$\dot{x}_1 = -\frac{B}{J}x_1 + \frac{K_t}{J}x_2$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** From the armature KVL $L_a \dot{i}_a + R_a i_a + K_b \omega = e_a$:
$$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{K_b}{L_a}x_1 - \frac{R_a}{L_a}x_2 + \frac{1}{L_a}e_a$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Assembled matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{B}{J} & \frac{K_t}{J} \\ -\frac{K_b}{L_a} & -\frac{R_a}{L_a} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \frac{1}{L_a} \end{bmatrix} e_a$$
$$y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify coefficients: $a_1 = 3, a_2 = 2$ (denominator); $b_1 = 1, b_2 = 4$ (numerator).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the differential equation:
$$\ddot{y} + 3\dot{y} + 2y = \dot{u} + 4u$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Choose phase variables $x_1 = y, x_2 = \dot{y}$. Then:
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -2x_1 - 3x_2 + u$$ (absorbing the $\dot{u}$ into the output equation)

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Output: $y = x_1$ plus the input-derivative correction. Strict phase variable canonical form:
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = 0$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Eigenvalues from $\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = (\lambda+1)(\lambda+2) = 0$:
$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = -1, \quad \lambda_2 = -2}$$

Both eigenvalues are real and negative → system is **overdamped and stable**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong state variable selection (–2 marks):** Choosing resistor voltage or inductor voltage as state instead of energy-storing element current/voltage.
> 2. **Sign errors in KVL/Newton's law (–1 to –2 marks):** A single sign mistake in the back-EMF term ($+K_b \omega$ vs $-K_b \omega$) flips the entire stability conclusion.
> 3. **Forgetting to express output in terms of states (–1 mark):** The output equation $y = \mathbf{C}x + \mathbf{D}u$ is *mandatory*; many students write only the state equation.
> 4. **Incorrect ordering of phase variable coefficients (–2 marks):** The bottom row of $\mathbf{A}$ is $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$, NOT $[-a_1, -a_2, \ldots, -a_n]$. Writing them in the wrong order produces a different (wrong) system.
> 5. **Mixing up $K_t$ and $K_b$ (–1 mark):** In SI units, $K_t$ (Nm/A) and $K_b$ (V·s/rad) are numerically equal but dimensionally distinct; do not drop units.
> 6. **Missing $D$ matrix:** Always state $D = 0$ for strictly proper systems; omitting it costs a mark.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **State Variable** = minimal memory of a system (energy-storing quantities: $i_L, v_C, \omega, i_a$).
- **State Vector** $\mathbf{x}$ packs all state variables into one column.
- **RLC Circuit Standard A-Matrix:**
$$\mathbf{A}_{RLC} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \tfrac{1}{C} \\ -\tfrac{1}{L} & -\tfrac{R}{L} \end{bmatrix}$$
- **DC Motor Standard A-Matrix:**
$$\mathbf{A}_{DC} = \begin{bmatrix} -\tfrac{B}{J} & \tfrac{K_t}{J} \\ -\tfrac{K_b}{L_a} & -\tfrac{R_a}{L_a} \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Phase Variable Form:** $x_i = y^{(i-1)}$; A is in companion form with 1's on superdiagonal.
- **Companion A Bottom Row:** $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$ where $a_i$ are denominator coefficients of the transfer function.
- **Phase Variable B:** Always $\mathbf{B}_{PV} = [0, \ldots, 0, 1]^T$.
- **Phase Variable C:** Numerator coefficients $[b_n, b_{n-1}, \ldots, b_0]$.
- **D Matrix:** Zero for strictly proper transfer functions (degree numerator < degree denominator).
- **Eigenvalues = Poles:** Determined by $\det(\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$.
- **Stability Criterion:** All eigenvalues must have **negative real parts** for asymptotic stability.
- **Number of State Variables** = Order of the system = Number of independent energy storage elements.
- **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Questions on phase variable form, RLC state-space, and DC motor modeling appear **every semester** in Module 1 — allocate at least 40% of your Module 1 preparation here.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diagonal Canonical Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT635 - ADVANCED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diagonal Canonical Forms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF)** is a state-space representation of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system in which the system matrix **A** is a **diagonal matrix**. The state variables in this form are completely **decoupled**, and each diagonal element of **A** represents an **eigenvalue (characteristic root)** of the system.

The general state-space representation in Diagonal Canonical Form is given by:

$$
\dot{X}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix} X(t) + \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ \vdots \\ b_n \end{bmatrix} U(t)
$$

$$
Y(t) = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & c_2 & \cdots & c_n \end{bmatrix} X(t) + dU(t)
$$

Where $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$ are the **distinct eigenvalues** of the system matrix.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Orchestra Conductor and the Soloists"**
>
> Imagine a large orchestra where every musician is playing at their own tempo, causing chaos. Now imagine hiring a **conductor (the Transformation Matrix T)** who reorganizes the orchestra into independent soloists, each playing their own melody separately.
>
> - **Coupled System (CCF/OCF):** Every musician depends on the others — complex, hard to analyze.
> - **Diagonal Canonical Form:** The conductor transforms the music into **N independent solo performances**. Each soloist (state variable) plays **completely on their own** — easy to analyze, modify, and control.
>
> The conductor's job is performed mathematically by the **modal transformation matrix**, built from the **eigenvectors** of the original system matrix **A**.

This decoupling allows engineers to:
- **Analyze each mode independently** (a critical advantage in vibration, aerospace, and robotics).
- **Design controllers** for individual modes.
- **Predict stability** by simply checking the sign of diagonal entries (eigenvalues).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The transformation matrix **T** is constructed from the **linearly independent eigenvectors** of **A**.
> - The Diagonal Canonical Form exists **only if all eigenvalues of A are distinct (no repeated roots)**. For repeated roots, the **Jordan Canonical Form** is used instead.
> - **Stability Rule:** A system in DCF is **asymptotically stable** if and only if **all diagonal entries** $\lambda_i$ have **negative real parts** (i.e., lie in the left-half of the s-plane).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Diagonal Canonical Form Exists

The Diagonal Canonical Form arises from the **Modal Decomposition** of a linear system. The key insight is the **Spectral Theorem** of linear algebra:

> If a matrix **A** (of size $n \times n$) has **n linearly independent eigenvectors**, then there exists an invertible transformation matrix **T** such that:
> $$A_d = T^{-1} A T$$
> where $A_d$ is a **diagonal matrix** of eigenvalues.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Modal Transformation

1. **Step 1 — Find Eigenvalues:** Solve the characteristic equation $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$ to obtain the $n$ roots $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$.

2. **Step 2 — Find Eigenvectors:** For each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$, solve $A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ to obtain eigenvector $v_i$.

3. **Step 3 — Construct T:** Form the modal matrix $T = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 & v_2 & \cdots & v_n \end{bmatrix}$.

4. **Step 4 — Apply Transformation:** Use the state transformation $X = T Z$ (where $Z$ is the new decoupled state vector). Substitute into the original state equation:
   $$T \dot{Z} = A T Z + B U$$
   $$\dot{Z} = T^{-1} A T Z + T^{-1} B U$$
   $$\dot{Z} = A_d Z + B_d U$$

5. **Step 5 — Verify Diagonality:** $A_d = T^{-1} A T = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n)$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU Module 1 questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | State Transformation | $X(t) = T \cdot Z(t)$ | $T$ is the modal matrix of eigenvectors |
| 2 | Transformed A matrix | $A_d = T^{-1} A T$ | Always diagonal when eigenvectors are independent |
| 3 | Transformed B matrix | $B_d = T^{-1} B$ | Generally non-diagonal |
| 4 | Transformed C matrix | $C_d = C T$ | Generally non-diagonal |
| 5 | Diagonal entries | $A_d = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n)$ | Eigenvalues of original A |
| 6 | Eigenvector equation | $A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | Definition of eigenvector $v_i$ |
| 7 | Characteristic eqn | $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$ | Yields eigenvalues |
| 8 | Transfer function (DCF) | $G(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{c_i b_i}{s - \lambda_i}$ | Sum of decoupled first-order terms |
| 9 | Stability condition | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \;\; \forall i$ | All eigenvalues in LHP |
| 10 | Transfer function from TF | $G(s) = \frac{b_0 s^{n-1} + \cdots + b_{n-1}}{(s - \lambda_1)(s - \lambda_2) \cdots (s - \lambda_n)}$ | For distinct real poles |

> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** For absolute value or magnitude, write $\vert s - \lambda_i \vert$ in math mode (NOT using the vertical pipe `|` inside tables). This keeps markdown tables intact.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility / Real-World Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Diagonal Canonical Form is the **mathematical backbone of Modal Control Theory**:
>
> - **Aerospace:** Decoupling aircraft longitudinal and lateral dynamics for autopilot design.
> - **Power Systems:** Analyzing individual oscillatory modes in large interconnected grids.
> - **Structural Engineering:** Determining natural vibration modes of buildings and bridges.
> - **Robotics:** Identifying dominant motion modes in multi-DOF manipulators.
> - **Process Control:** Simplifying MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output) controller synthesis.

The **decoupled nature** allows engineers to design **independent SISO controllers** for each mode — a major simplification in real engineering projects.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Conversion of Transfer Function to Diagonal Canonical Form

### Given:
A single-input single-output (SISO) LTI system with transfer function having **distinct real poles**:
$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{b_0 s^{n-1} + b_1 s^{n-2} + \cdots + b_{n-2} s + b_{n-1}}{(s - \lambda_1)(s - \lambda_2) \cdots (s - \lambda_n)}$$

### Step 1 — Apply Partial Fraction Expansion

Express the transfer function as a sum of simple first-order terms. By partial fraction expansion:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K_1}{s - \lambda_1} + \frac{K_2}{s - \lambda_2} + \cdots + \frac{K_n}{s - \lambda_n}
$$

where the **residue** $K_i$ is computed as:

$$
K_i = \lim_{s \to \lambda_i} (s - \lambda_i) G(s)
$$

### Step 2 — Define Decoupled State Variables

For each diagonal term, define a state variable $z_i(s)$ such that:

$$
Z_i(s) = \frac{1}{s - \lambda_i} U(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{z}_i(t) = \lambda_i z_i(t) + u(t)
$$

The output is then:

$$
Y(s) = K_1 Z_1(s) + K_2 Z_2(s) + \cdots + K_n Z_n(s)
$$

### Step 3 — Write the State-Space in Matrix Form

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{z}_1 \\ \dot{z}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{z}_n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z_1 \\ z_2 \\ \vdots \\ z_n \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} U
$$

$$
Y = \begin{bmatrix} K_1 & K_2 & \cdots & K_n \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} z_1 \\ z_2 \\ \vdots \\ z_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **Diagonal Canonical Form** with $A_d$, $B_d$, $C_d$ matrices fully defined.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Construction of Transformation Matrix from Eigenvectors

### Given:
A system matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix}$ (this is a standard 2nd-order example).

### Step 1 — Compute Characteristic Polynomial

$$
\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 6 & \lambda + 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \lambda(\lambda + 5) - (-1)(6) = \lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6
$$

### Step 2 — Find Eigenvalues

Solve $\lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6 = 0$:

$$
(\lambda + 2)(\lambda + 3) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = -2, \;\; \lambda_2 = -3
$$

### Step 3 — Find Eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = -2$

Solve $(A - \lambda_1 I) v_1 = 0$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0 - (-2) & 1 \\ -6 & -5 - (-2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -6 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

From the first row: $2 v_{11} + v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{12} = -2 v_{11}$.

Choose $v_{11} = 1$, then $v_{12} = -2$:

$$
v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Find Eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = -3$

Solve $(A - \lambda_2 I) v_2 = 0$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0 - (-3) & 1 \\ -6 & -5 - (-3) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{21} \\ v_{22} \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -6 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{21} \\ v_{22} \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

From the first row: $3 v_{21} + v_{22} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{22} = -3 v_{21}$.

Choose $v_{21} = 1$, then $v_{22} = -3$:

$$
v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Form the Modal Matrix T

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 & v_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute $T^{-1}$

$$
\det(T) = (1)(-3) - (1)(-2) = -3 + 2 = -1
$$

$$
T^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1} \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 7 — Verify $A_d = T^{-1} A T$

$$
A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 7a — Multiply $A \cdot T$:

$$
A T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-2) & 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot (-3) \\ -6 \cdot 1 + (-5)(-2) & -6 \cdot 1 + (-5)(-3) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A T = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 4 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 7b — Multiply $T^{-1} \cdot (A T)$:

$$
A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -3 \\ 4 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 3(-2) + 1(4) & 3(-3) + 1(9) \\ -2(-2) + (-1)(4) & -2(-3) + (-1)(9) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
A_d = \begin{bmatrix} -6 + 4 & -9 + 9 \\ 4 - 4 & 6 - 9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Verification

$$
A_d = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark
$$

The system is now in **Diagonal Canonical Form**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Check:** Both $\lambda_1 = -2$ and $\lambda_2 = -3$ are **negative**, hence the system is **asymptotically stable**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Canonical Forms

| Feature | Controllable CF | Observable CF | Diagonal CF | Jordan CF |
|---------|---------------|---------------|-------------|-----------|
| A matrix | Companion form | Companion (transposed) form | Diagonal | Block diagonal |
| Decoupling | No | No | **Yes (full)** | Partial (per block) |
| Eigenvalues visible? | Yes (in last row) | Yes (in last column) | **Yes (on diagonal)** | Yes (on diagonal) |
| Requires distinct eigenvalues? | No | No | **Yes (mandatory)** | No (handles repeats) |
| State interpretation | Energy storage (chain) | Energy storage (chain) | **Independent modes** | Generalized modes |
| Preferred for | Controller design | Observer design | **Modal analysis** | Repeated root systems |
| KTU exam weight | High | High | **Very High** | Medium |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Diagonalization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original State-Space Model] --> B[Compute Characteristic Equation det lambda I minus A]
    B --> C[Extract Eigenvalues lambda 1, lambda 2, ..., lambda n]
    C --> D[For Each lambda i, Solve A v i = lambda i v i]
    D --> E[Construct Modal Matrix T from Eigenvectors]
    E --> F[Verify T is Invertible det T nonzero]
    F --> G[Apply State Transformation X = T Z]
    G --> H[Derive New Matrices: A d = T inv A T, B d = T inv B, C d = C T]
    H --> I[Diagonal Canonical Form Achieved]
    I --> J[Independent First-Order Subsystems]
    J --> K[Modal Analysis and Control Design]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#d4edda
    style K fill:#fff3cd
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Decoupled Diagonal System Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Input U s] --> M1[1 over s minus lambda 1]
    U --> M2[1 over s minus lambda 2]
    U --> M3[1 over s minus lambda n]
    M1 --> K1[Residue K 1]
    M2 --> K2[Residue K 2]
    M3 --> K3[Residue K n]
    K1 --> SUM[Sum Junction]
    K2 --> SUM
    K3 --> SUM
    SUM --> Y[Output Y s]
    
    style M1 fill:#cce5ff
    style M2 fill:#cce5ff
    style M3 fill:#cce5ff
    style SUM fill:#ffe1e1
    style Y fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.3 Transformation Flow — Original vs. Diagonal Form

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Original["Original Coupled System"]
        X1[State x1] -->|a12| X2[State x2]
        X2 -->|a21| X1
        X1 -->|a13| X3[State x3]
        X3 -->|a31| X1
    end
    
    subgraph Transform["Modal Transformation X = T Z"]
        T1[Modal Matrix T] 
    end
    
    subgraph Diagonal["Decoupled Diagonal Form"]
        Z1[Mode z1 lambda 1] 
        Z2[Mode z2 lambda 2]
        Z3[Mode z3 lambda 3]
    end
    
    Original -->|Apply T| Transform
    Transform -->|Result| Diagonal
    
    style Original fill:#ffe1e1
    style Diagonal fill:#d4edda
    style Transform fill:#fff3cd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.3:** The original system has **interconnected states** ($x_1$ affects $x_2$, $x_2$ affects $x_1$, etc.). After applying the modal transformation $T$, the system becomes three **completely independent** modes, each evolving at its own characteristic rate $\lambda_i$.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Modeled after:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (2-mark scaled to 3-mark KTU internal style)

### **Q1. Define Diagonal Canonical Form. When does it exist?**

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]:**

> Diagonal Canonical Form is a state-space representation in which the system matrix **A** is a **diagonal matrix** $A_d = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n)$, where $\lambda_i$ are the eigenvalues of the original system.
>
> **Existence Condition:** It exists **if and only if** the system matrix **A has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors**, which is guaranteed when **all eigenvalues of A are distinct** (no repeated roots).
>
> **Significance:** The form decouples the state equations, allowing independent modal analysis and control design. **(1 mark each for: definition, condition, significance)**

---

### **Q2. What is a modal matrix? How is it constructed?**

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]:**

> A **modal matrix T** is an invertible matrix whose columns are the **linearly independent eigenvectors** of the system matrix A.
>
> **Construction Steps:**
> 1. Solve $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$ to find eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$.
> 2. For each $\lambda_i$, solve $(A - \lambda_i I) v_i = 0$ to find eigenvector $v_i$.
> 3. Form $T = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 & v_2 & \cdots & v_n \end{bmatrix}$.
>
> **Application:** $A_d = T^{-1} A T$ produces the diagonal form. **(1.5 marks for definition + construction, 1.5 marks for the application equation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Modeled after:** `[KTU University Exam - December 2023]` (End-Semester Examination Pattern)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
For the system described by the transfer function:
$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{2s + 7}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$$

Obtain the **Diagonal Canonical Form** state-space representation. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
A system has the state-space matrices:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -8 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Transform it into Diagonal Canonical Form using the modal matrix. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(a)**

**Step 1 — Identify the eigenvalues from poles of $G(s)$:**
The poles are at $s = -1, -2, -3$, so $\lambda_1 = -1, \lambda_2 = -2, \lambda_3 = -3$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Apply partial fraction expansion:**
$$G(s) = \frac{2s + 7}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{K_1}{s+1} + \frac{K_2}{s+2} + \frac{K_3}{s+3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute residues (using cover-up method):**

For $K_1$: substitute $s = -1$:
$$K_1 = \left. \frac{2s + 7}{(s+2)(s+3)} \right|_{s = -1} = \frac{2(-1) + 7}{(-1+2)(-1+3)} = \frac{5}{(1)(2)} = \frac{5}{2}$$

For $K_2$: substitute $s = -2$:
$$K_2 = \left. \frac{2s + 7}{(s+1)(s+3)} \right|_{s = -2} = \frac{2(-2) + 7}{(-2+1)(-2+3)} = \frac{3}{(-1)(1)} = -3$$

For $K_3$: substitute $s = -3$:
$$K_3 = \left. \frac{2s + 7}{(s+1)(s+2)} \right|_{s = -3} = \frac{2(-3) + 7}{(-3+1)(-3+2)} = \frac{1}{(-2)(-1)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Write the state equations from the partial fractions:**
Define $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3$ such that:
$$\dot{z}_1 = -z_1 + u, \quad \dot{z}_2 = -2 z_2 + u, \quad \dot{z}_3 = -3 z_3 + u$$

**Step 5 — Construct the matrices:** **(2 marks)**

$$
A_d = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_d = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_d = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{5}{2} & -3 & \frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Stability check:** All $\lambda_i < 0$ → **System is stable.** ✓ **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(b)**

**Step 1 — Find eigenvalues of A:**
$$\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 8 & \lambda + 6 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda+6) + 8 = \lambda^2 + 6\lambda + 8$$

Solving: $\lambda^2 + 6\lambda + 8 = 0 \Rightarrow (\lambda+2)(\lambda+4) = 0$

So $\lambda_1 = -2, \lambda_2 = -4$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Find eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = -2$:**
$$(A + 2I) v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -8 & -4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} \\ v_{12} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

From row 1: $2 v_{11} + v_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{12} = -2 v_{11}$. Choose $v_{11} = 1$: $v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Find eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = -4$:**
$$(A + 4I) v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ -8 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{21} \\ v_{22} \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

From row 1: $4 v_{21} + v_{22} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{22} = -4 v_{21}$. Choose $v_{21} = 1$: $v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -4 \end{bmatrix}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Construct T and compute $T^{-1}$:** **(1 mark)**
$$T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(T) = -4 - (-2) = -2$$

$$T^{-1} = \frac{1}{-2} \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0.5 \\ -1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $A_d = T^{-1} A T$:** **(2 marks)**

First, $A T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -8 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -4 \\ 4 & 16 \end{bmatrix}$

Then, $A_d = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0.5 \\ -1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -4 \\ 4 & 16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 + 2 & -8 + 8 \\ 2 - 2 & 4 - 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$ ✓

**Step 6 — Compute $B_d$ and $C_d$:** **(1 mark)**

$$B_d = T^{-1} B = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0.5 \\ -1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C_d = C T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Diagonal Canonical Form:**
$$
A_d = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_d = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Explain the **limitations of Diagonal Canonical Form**. When is **Jordan Canonical Form** preferred? Derive the relationship between Diagonal Form and **Transfer Function**. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
For the system $G(s) = \dfrac{5(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)^2}$, explain why the Diagonal Canonical Form **cannot** be directly obtained. Sketch the appropriate alternative form. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a)**

**Limitations of DCF (3 marks):**

1. **Requires distinct eigenvalues:** If A has repeated eigenvalues (algebraic multiplicity > geometric multiplicity), then $A$ is not diagonalizable, and DCF cannot be constructed.
2. **Numerical sensitivity:** The transformation matrix T can be ill-conditioned when eigenvalues are close together, leading to numerical instability in computations.
3. **No generalization to defective systems:** For matrices with insufficient linearly independent eigenvectors (defective matrices), the diagonal form does not exist.

**When to use Jordan Canonical Form (2 marks):**

Jordan Canonical Form is preferred when:
- The system has **repeated eigenvalues**.
- The geometric multiplicity is less than the algebraic multiplicity.
- The system matrix is **defective** (i.e., it has fewer than $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors).

In Jordan form, the A matrix becomes block-diagonal with each block corresponding to a repeated eigenvalue, containing 1's on the superdiagonal.

**Transfer Function Derivation (2 marks):**

In DCF, since $A_d$ is diagonal, we can write each decoupled equation as:
$$s Z_i(s) = \lambda_i Z_i(s) + U(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_i(s) = \frac{U(s)}{s - \lambda_i}$$

The output becomes:
$$Y(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i Z_i(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{c_i b_i}{s - \lambda_i} U(s)$$

Hence the transfer function is:
$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{c_i b_i}{s - \lambda_i}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b)**

**Step 1 — Analyze the poles:** **(2 marks)**
The transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{5(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)^2}$ has:
- A **simple pole** at $s = -1$.
- A **repeated pole** at $s = -3$ with multiplicity **2**.

**Step 2 — Why DCF fails:** **(2 marks)**
Diagonal Canonical Form requires **all eigenvalues to be distinct**. Since $s = -3$ is a repeated eigenvalue, the system matrix in DCF would require three diagonal entries, but two of them must be the same ($-3$). This violates the requirement for $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors of $A$. The matrix would be **defective** (not fully diagonalizable).

**Step 3 — Required approach:** **(2 marks)**
The system must be represented in **Jordan Canonical Form** with a $2 \times 2$ Jordan block for the repeated eigenvalue $-3$:

$$
A_J = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -3 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Sketch the Jordan form structure:** **(1 mark)**
The system can be written as:
- Mode 1: $\dot{z}_1 = -z_1 + u$ (independent, eigenvalue $-1$).
- Mode 2: $\dot{z}_2 = -3 z_2 + z_3$ (coupled within Jordan block).
- Mode 3: $\dot{z}_3 = -3 z_3 + u$.

The off-diagonal "1" in the Jordan block captures the **generalized eigenvector** effect that arises due to the repeated pole.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify invertibility of T:** Always check that $\det(T) \neq 0$. A non-invertible T means the eigenvectors are linearly dependent → **Diagonal form does not exist** (lose 2 marks).
>
> 2. **Sign errors in eigenvalue computation:** For system with $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\omega_n^2 & -2\zeta\omega_n \end{bmatrix}$, students often confuse signs when forming $(\lambda I - A)$. Always double-check the characteristic equation expansion.
>
> 3. **Misapplying the transformation:** The correct form is $A_d = T^{-1} A T$, **NOT** $A_d = T A T^{-1}$. Reversing the order gives incorrect (non-diagonal) results.
>
> 4. **Assuming DCF works for repeated poles:** If you see a repeated pole in a transfer function (like $(s+3)^2$), **immediately switch to Jordan Canonical Form**. Attempting DCF here is a guaranteed 0 for the answer.
>
> 5. **Skipping the stability check:** Always end your solution with: "Since all $\lambda_i < 0$, the system is **asymptotically stable**." Examiners allocate 1 mark for this statement.
>
> 6. **Not showing the residue calculation:** In Q-A(a), partial fraction residues must be **explicitly shown** using the cover-up method, not just stated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Diagonal Canonical Forms**

- ✅ **Definition:** State-space form where $A$ matrix is **diagonal** with eigenvalues on the diagonal.
- ✅ **Existence:** Requires **distinct eigenvalues** (n linearly independent eigenvectors).
- ✅ **Transformation:** $X = T Z$, where $T$ is the **modal matrix** of eigenvectors.
- ✅ **Key Equation:** $A_d = T^{-1} A T$ (verify by expanding — must give a diagonal matrix).
- ✅ **Eigenvector Equation:** $A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ — solve for each eigenvalue.
- ✅ **Modal Matrix Construction:** $T = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 & v_2 & \cdots & v_n \end{bmatrix}$.
- ✅ **Partial Fraction Approach:** For DCF from $G(s)$, perform PFE — residues become entries of $C_d$.
- ✅ **Transfer Function in DCF:** $G(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{c_i b_i}{s - \lambda_i}$ (sum of first-order terms).
- ✅ **Stability Rule:** All diagonal entries $\lambda_i$ must have **negative real parts** (LHP).
- ✅ **Limitation:** Fails for **repeated eigenvalues** → use **Jordan Canonical Form** instead.
- ✅ **B matrix in DCF from TF:** Always a column of **ones** (since $u$ feeds each decoupled mode directly).
- ✅ **C matrix in DCF from TF:** Contains the **residues** $K_i$ from partial fraction expansion.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always explicitly verify the final $A_d$ is diagonal by matrix multiplication.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** State "$\det(T) \neq 0$" after computing $T$ to confirm invertibility.
- ✅ **Real-world application:** Modal analysis in aerospace, structural dynamics, and MIMO control.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Similarity Transformations to Diagonal Canonical Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT635 - ADVANCED CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Similarity Transformations to Diagonal Canonical Form # 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Modern Control Theory**, a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) continuous-time system in state-space form is described as: $$ \dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) $$ $$ y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t) $$ where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$, and $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Similarity Transformation (Algebraic Definition)** > A **similarity transformation** is a change of coordinates defined by a nonsingular (invertible) matrix $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ such that: > $$\bar{x}(t) = P^{-1} x(t) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad x(t) = P \, \bar{x}(t)$$ > When applied to the state-space system, the transformed matrices are: > $$\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P, \quad \bar{B} = P^{-1} B, \quad \bar{C} = C P, \quad \bar{D} = D$$ > The new system $\dot{\bar{x}} = \bar{A}\bar{x} + \bar{B}u$ is said to be **algebraically equivalent** to the original. ## 1.2 Diagonal Canonical Form (DCF) > [!NOTE] > **Diagonal Canonical Form Definition** > A system $\{A, B, C, D\}$ is in **Diagonal Canonical Form** if the system matrix $A$ is a diagonal matrix: > $$\bar{A} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n) = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix}$$ > where $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$ are the **eigenvalues** of the original matrix $A$. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rotation of Axes" Viewpoint Imagine you are standing in a slanted, messy coordinate room with tilted walls. The room contains a complex object (the system dynamics). To study it, you walk into a clean, perfectly aligned coordinate room where the walls are at right angles and the object's shape becomes immediately recognizable. - The **slanted room** is the original state-space basis — coupled and confusing. - The **clean room** is the **diagonal canonical form** — each axis acts independently. - The act of "walking from one room to the other" is the **similarity transformation** $P$.
