In the diagonal room, the equations of motion **decouple** — each state variable evolves independently according to its own eigenvalue, just like three independent pendulums hanging from a single frame once their axes are aligned. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!TIP] > **Key Symbols Used in KTU Boards** > - **$n$** — Order of the system (number of state variables). > - **$m$** — Number of inputs. > - **$p$** — Number of outputs. > - **$\lambda_i$** — $i^{th}$ eigenvalue of $A$. > - **$v_i$** — $i^{th}$ eigenvector of $A$. > - **$P$** — Modal matrix (transformation matrix whose columns are eigenvectors). > - **$\sigma(A)$** — Spectrum (set of eigenvalues) of $A$. # 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Diagonalize? The state equation $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ is a **coupled** set of $n$ first-order ODEs. Coupling makes: - Analytical solution difficult (matrix exponentials are complex). - Modal analysis (understanding natural modes) hidden. - Controller design (e.g., pole placement) non-intuitive. Diagonalization **decouples** the system into $n$ **independent scalar ODEs**: $$\dot{\bar{x}}_i(t) = \lambda_i \bar{x}_i(t) + \bar{b}_i u(t), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ Each mode can now be studied, designed, or controlled in isolation — a massive simplification. ## 2.2 Mathematical Conditions for Diagonalization A matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is **diagonalizable** if and only if: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Existence of $n$ Linearly Independent Eigenvectors** > A matrix $A$ is diagonalizable over $\mathbb{C}$ if and only if it has a complete set of $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors. This is guaranteed if and only if: > 1. **All eigenvalues are distinct** (sufficient condition), OR > 2. **The geometric multiplicity equals the algebraic multiplicity for every eigenvalue** (necessary and sufficient). If the conditions are not met, the system can still be brought to a **Jordan Canonical Form** (JCF), which is the closest diagonalizable approximation. ## 2.3 Construction of the Transformation Matrix $P$ (Modal Matrix) Let $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ be the linearly independent eigenvectors of $A$ corresponding to eigenvalues $\{\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n\}$. Then the **modal matrix** is constructed as: $$P = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \cdots \mid v_n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v_{11} & v_{12} & \cdots & v_{1n} \\ v_{21} & v_{22} & \cdots & v_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ v_{n1} & v_{n2} & \cdots & v_{nn} \end{bmatrix}$$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Property | Expression | Notes / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | State Transformation | $\bar{x} = P^{-1} x$ | $\bar{x}, x \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | | 2 | Transformed $A$-matrix | $\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P$ | Must be diagonal | | 3 | Transformed $B$-matrix | $\bar{B} = P^{-1} B$ | Input coupling matrix | | 4 | Transformed $C$-matrix | $\bar{C} = C P$ | Output coupling matrix | | 5 | Diagonal $A$-matrix | $\bar{A} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$ | Eigenvalues on diagonal | | 6 | Eigenvector Equation | $A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ | Definition of eigenpair | | 7 | Characteristic Polynomial | $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$ | Yields eigenvalues | | 8 | Invariant: Trace | $\text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Sum of eigenvalues | | 9 | Invariant: Determinant | $\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Product of eigenvalues | | 10 | Invariant: Transfer Function | $C(sI - A)^{-1}B = \bar{C}(sI - \bar{A})^{-1}\bar{B}$ | System input-output behavior preserved | | 11 | Solution in $\bar{x}$ coordinates | $\bar{x}(t) = e^{\bar{A} t} \bar{x}(0) + \int_0^t e^{\bar{A}(t-\tau)} \bar{B} u(\tau) d\tau$ | $e^{\bar{A} t} = \text{diag}(e^{\lambda_1 t}, \ldots, e^{\lambda_n t})$ | | 12 | Inverse Modal Matrix | $P^{-1} = \dfrac{\text{adj}(P)}{\det(P)}$ | Valid when $\det(P) \neq 0$ | ## 2.5 Invariant Properties Preserved Under Similarity Transformation A similarity transformation preserves the **system's physical identity**. The following are **invariant** (unchanged) under $\bar{A} = P^{-1}AP$: 1. **Eigenvalues** (spectrum $\sigma(A) = \sigma(\bar{A})$) 2. **Characteristic polynomial** 3. **Trace** of $A$ 4. **Determinant** of $A$ 5. **Rank** of $A$ 6. **Transfer function** (input-output behavior) 7. **Controllability** and **Observability** 8. **Stability** (location of poles in $s$-plane) 9. **Minimal polynomial** ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Why Engineers Care About Diagonal Form** > - **Vibration Analysis**: Decouples structural modes of buildings, aircraft wings, and bridges. > - **Power Systems**: Decouples synchronous generator swing equations. > - **Aircraft Dynamics**: Separates short-period and phugoid modes for clean control design. > - **Modal Control**: State feedback gains $K$ can be designed one-mode-at-a-time in the diagonal basis. # 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From State Equation to Diagonal Form **Given:** Original system $$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) \quad (1)$$ **Step 1 — Apply the transformation.** Let $x(t) = P \bar{x}(t)$. Substituting into (1): $$P \dot{\bar{x}}(t) = A P \bar{x}(t) + B u(t) \quad (2)$$ **Step 2 — Pre-multiply by $P^{-1}$** to isolate $\dot{\bar{x}}$: $$P^{-1} P \dot{\bar{x}}(t) = P^{-1} A P \bar{x}(t) + P^{-1} B u(t)$$ Since $P^{-1}P = I$ (identity matrix): $$\dot{\bar{x}}(t) = \underbrace{P^{-1} A P}_{\bar{A}} \bar{x}(t) + \underbrace{P^{-1} B}_{\bar{B}} u(t) \quad (3)$$ **Step 3 — Choose $P$ such that $\bar{A}$ is diagonal.** Since $A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$, we may write this in matrix form as: $$A \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \cdots \mid v_n \end{bmatrix}}_{P} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \cdots \mid v_n \end{bmatrix}}_{P} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{diag}(\lambda_i)}$$ That is, $AP = P \Lambda$. Pre-multiplying by $P^{-1}$: $$\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P = \Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n) \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Complete Solved Example (2x2 System) — KTU Board Pattern **Problem.** Consider the system: $$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} u$$ Convert it to diagonal canonical form using similarity transformation. ### Step 1 — Compute Eigenvalues of $A$ Characteristic equation: $$\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda - 1 & -2 \\ -3 & \lambda - 2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$ $$ \begin{aligned} (\lambda - 1)(\lambda - 2) - (-2)(-3) &= 0 \\ \lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 2 - 6 &= 0 \\ \lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 4 &= 0 \\ (\lambda - 4)(\lambda + 1) &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, $\lambda_1 = 4$ and $\lambda_2 = -1$. Since both are **distinct**, diagonalization is guaranteed. ### Step 2 — Compute Eigenvectors **For $\lambda_1 = 4$:** Solve $(A - 4I) v_1 = 0$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 4 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 - 4 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ From the first row: $-3 v_{11} + 2 v_{21} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{21} = \tfrac{3}{2} v_{11}$. Choose $v_{11} = 2$, then $v_{21} = 3$. $$ v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} $$ **For $\lambda_2 = -1$:** Solve $(A + I) v_2 = 0$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 + 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 + 1 \end{bmatrix} v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix} v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ From the first row: $2 v_{12} + 2 v_{22} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{22} = -v_{12}$. Choose $v_{12} = 1$, then $v_{22} = -1$. $$ v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 3 — Form the Modal Matrix $P$ $$ P = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \mid v_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute $P^{-1}$ $$ \det(P) = (2)(-1) - (1)(3) = -2 - 3 = -5 $$ $$ P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-5} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 & 1/5 \\ 3/5 & -2/5 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute $\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P$ First compute $AP$: $$ AP = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (1)(2)+(2)(3) & (1)(1)+(2)(-1) \\ (3)(2)+(2)(3) & (3)(1)+(2)(-1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Then compute $P^{-1}(AP)$: $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 & 1/5 \\ 3/5 & -2/5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} (1/5)(8) + (1/5)(12) & (1/5)(-1) + (1/5)(1) \\ (3/5)(8) + (-2/5)(12) & (3/5)(-1) + (-2/5)(1) \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 8/5 + 12/5 & -1/5 + 1/5 \\ 24/5 - 24/5 & -3/5 - 2/5 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **Diagonal Canonical Form** with eigenvalues on the diagonal. ✓ ### Step 6 — Compute $\bar{B} = P^{-1} B$ $$ \bar{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 & 1/5 \\ 3/5 & -2/5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 \\ 3/5 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Final Transformed System $$ \boxed{\; \dot{\bar{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \bar{x} + \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 \\ 3/5 \end{bmatrix} u \;} $$ This represents two completely **decoupled** first-order systems: $$ \dot{\bar{x}}_1 = 4 \bar{x}_1 + \tfrac{1}{5} u, \qquad \dot{\bar{x}}_2 = -\bar{x}_2 + \tfrac{3}{5} u $$ ### Verification — Trace and Determinant Invariance - $\text{tr}(A) = 1 + 2 = 3$, $\text{tr}(\bar{A}) = 4 + (-1) = 3$ ✓ - $\det(A) = (1)(2) - (2)(3) = -4$, $\det(\bar{A}) = (4)(-1) = -4$ ✓ ## 3.3 General Algorithm (For $n \times n$ Systems) 1. **Find eigenvalues** by solving $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$. 2. **Verify diagonalizability** (distinct eigenvalues ⇒ OK). 3. **For each $\lambda_i$**, solve $(A - \lambda_i I) v_i = 0$ to get eigenvector $v_i$. 4. **Construct** $P = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \cdots \mid v_n \end{bmatrix}$. 5. **Compute** $P^{-1}$ using adjugate, Gauss-Jordan, or numerical methods. 6. **Apply** $\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P$, $\bar{B} = P^{-1} B$, $\bar{C} = C P$, $\bar{D} = D$. # 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Original vs. Transformed System ```mermaid flowchart LR uIn["Input u(t)"] --> Bgain1["Matrix B"] Bgain1 --> SumAdd(("+")) Abox["Matrix A nn"] --> SumAdd SumAdd --> xdot["State Derivative x dot"] xdot --> IntBlock["Integrator"] IntBlock --> xout["State x(t)"] xout --> Cbox["Matrix C"] Cbox --> yout["Output y(t)"] xout --> Abox Trans["Transformation P"] -.-> xout TransInv["Inverse P inverse"] -.-> xout ``` ## 4.2 Algorithmic Flow: Diagonalization Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart(["Start: Given system A,B,C,D"]) --> nodeA["Step 1: Compute eigenvalues of A
Solve det lambda I minus A equals 0"] nodeA --> nodeB{"Step 2: Check diagonalizability
n linearly independent eigenvectors?"} nodeB -- "Yes" --> nodeC["Step 3: For each lambda sub i
Solve A minus lambda sub i I times v sub i equals 0"] nodeB -- "No" --> nodeJ["Use Jordan Canonical Form
JCF instead"] nodeC --> nodeD["Step 4: Form modal matrix P
Columns are eigenvectors v sub i"] nodeD --> nodeE["Step 5: Compute P inverse
Use adjugate or numerical method"] nodeE --> nodeF["Step 6: Compute A bar equals P inverse A P"] nodeF --> nodeG["Step 7: Compute B bar equals P inverse B"] nodeG --> nodeH["Step 8: Compute C bar equals C P"] nodeH --> nodeI["Step 9: Verify invariants
Trace, Determinant, Eigenvalues"] nodeI --> nodeEnd(["End: System in Diagonal Canonical Form"]) nodeJ --> nodeEnd ``` ## 4.3 Mapping: From Original Coupled System to Decoupled Form ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph subOrig["Original Coupled System"] dirA1["x1 dot depends on x1 and x2"] dirA2["x2 dot depends on x1 and x2"] end subOrig --> nodeTrans["Apply Similarity Transformation
x bar equals P inverse x"] nodeTrans --> subgraph subNew["Diagonal Canonical Form"] direction LR dirB1["x bar 1 dot equals lambda 1 x bar 1 plus input"] dirB2["x bar 2 dot equals lambda 2 x bar 2 plus input"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > The above block diagrams illustrate that the **physical system** (the arrows representing signal flow and energy transfer) remains unchanged — only the **mathematical representation** is simplified. # 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define similarity transformation. State the condition under which a system matrix $A$ can be diagonalized.** **Model Answer:** A similarity transformation is a change of state coordinates defined by $\bar{x} = P^{-1} x$ where $P$ is a nonsingular matrix. Under this transformation, the system matrix becomes $\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P$. A matrix $A$ can be diagonalized if and only if it possesses **$n$ linearly independent eigenvectors** (guaranteed when all eigenvalues are distinct). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **What is a modal matrix? How is it constructed?** **Model Answer:** A modal matrix $P$ is a nonsingular matrix whose columns are the linearly independent eigenvectors of $A$. It is constructed as $P = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \cdots \mid v_n \end{bmatrix}$ where $A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$. When used in similarity transformation, $P^{-1} A P$ yields a diagonal matrix of eigenvalues. --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **(a)** Consider the system $\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u$. Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the system matrix, and construct the modal matrix $P$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Using the modal matrix from part (a), transform the system into diagonal canonical form and write the decoupled state equations. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Step 1 — Eigenvalues:** $$ \det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda - 5 & -4 \\ -1 & \lambda - 2 \end{bmatrix} = 0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} (\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) - 4 &= 0 \\ \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 - 4 &= 0 \\ \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 6 &= 0 \\ (\lambda - 1)(\lambda - 6) &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 1$, $\lambda_2 = 6$. **Step 2 — Eigenvectors:** For $\lambda_1 = 1$: $(A - I) v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$ For $\lambda_2 = 6$: $(A - 6I) v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 1 & -4 \end{bmatrix} v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ **Step 3 — Modal Matrix:** $$ P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ #### **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Step 1 — Inverse:** $$ \det(P) = (1)(1) - (4)(-1) = 1 + 4 = 5 $$ $$ P^{-1} = \frac{1}{5} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 & -4/5 \\ 1/5 & 1/5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Diagonal $\bar{A}$:** $$ AP = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 4 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 24 \\ -1 & 6 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \bar{A} = P^{-1} A P = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 & -4/5 \\ 1/5 & 1/5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 24 \\ -1 & 6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — $\bar{B}$:** $$ \bar{B} = P^{-1} B = \begin{bmatrix} 1/5 & -4/5 \\ 1/5 & 1/5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3/5 \\ 2/5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Final Transformed System:** $$ \dot{\bar{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \bar{x} + \begin{bmatrix} -3/5 \\ 2/5 \end{bmatrix} u $$ Decoupled scalar equations: $$ \dot{\bar{x}}_1 = \bar{x}_1 - \tfrac{3}{5} u, \qquad \dot{\bar{x}}_2 = 6 \bar{x}_2 + \tfrac{2}{5} u $$ --- ### **Question B — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **(a)** For the system matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, determine the eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and the modal transformation matrix $P$. Verify that $P$ is non-singular. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Hence or otherwise, obtain the diagonal canonical form of the system with $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$. Also verify the trace and determinant invariance properties. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Step 1 — Eigenvalues:** $$ \det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 2 & \lambda + 3 \end{bmatrix} = 0 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda(\lambda + 3) + 2 &= 0 \\ \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 &= 0 \\ (\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 2) &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = -1$, $\lambda_2 = -2$. **Step 2 — Eigenvectors:** For $\lambda_1 = -1$: $(A + I) v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$ For $\lambda_2 = -2$: $(A + 2I) v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}$ **Step 3 — Modal Matrix:** $$ P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \det(P) = (1)(-2) - (1)(-1) = -2 + 1 = -1 \neq 0 $$ $P$ is non-singular. ✓ #### **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Step 1 — Inverse:** $$ P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1} \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — $\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P$:** First, $AP$: $$ AP = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -2 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} $$ Then, $P^{-1} A P$: $$ \bar{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -2 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — $\bar{B}$ and $\bar{C}$:** $$ \bar{B} = P^{-1} B = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \bar{C} = C P = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Final Diagonal System:** $$ \dot{\bar{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \bar{x} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} u, \qquad y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \bar{x} $$ **Verification of Invariants:** - Trace: $\text{tr}(A) = 0 + (-3) = -3$; $\text{tr}(\bar{A}) = -1 + (-2) = -3$ ✓ - Determinant: $\det(A) = (0)(-3) - (1)(-2) = 2$; $\det(\bar{A}) = (-1)(-2) = 2$ ✓ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Wrong eigenvector scaling:** Any non-zero scalar multiple of an eigenvector is valid. Marks are NOT deducted for different scaling, BUT you must show the equation $A v_i = \lambda_i v_i$ explicitly to claim full credit. > 2. **Confusing $\bar{B}$ and $\bar{C}$ formulas:** A common mistake is writing $\bar{B} = P B P^{-1}$. **Correct**: $\bar{B} = P^{-1} B$ and $\bar{C} = C P$. > 3. **Forgetting to verify $\det(P) \neq 0$:** Examiners award a step-mark for confirming $P$ is non-singular. > 4. **Arithmetic errors in $P^{-1}$:** Compute $P^{-1}$ using either cofactor/adjugate or by row-reduction. KTU expects a clean intermediate expression. > 5. **Ignoring Jordan form case:** If eigenvalues are **repeated**, the matrix is **not diagonalizable** and you must mention Jordan Canonical Form for full credit. --- ## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Similarity transformation** $\bar{x} = P^{-1} x$ preserves all physical and I/O properties of the system. - ✅ **Diagonal Canonical Form** is achieved when $\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)$. - ✅ **Modal matrix** $P$ has eigenvectors of $A$ as its **columns** (in order of eigenvalues). - ✅ **Diagonalizability** is guaranteed if and only if $A$ has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors (distinct eigenvalues ⇒ sufficient). - ✅ Transformed matrices: $\bar{A} = P^{-1} A P$, $\bar{B} = P^{-1} B$, $\bar{C} = C P$, $\bar{D} = D$. - ✅ **Invariants** under similarity: trace, determinant, eigenvalues, characteristic polynomial, rank, transfer function, controllability, observability, stability. - ✅ **Solution in DCF** becomes trivial: $\bar{x}_i(t) = e^{\lambda_i t} \bar{x}_i(0) + \int_0^t e^{\lambda_i (t-\tau)} \bar{b}_i u(\tau) d\tau$. - ✅ **Repeated eigenvalues** ⇒ may lead to Jordan blocks (JCF) — not pure DCF. - ✅ **For board exams**, always show: (1) characteristic equation, (2) eigenvectors, (3) modal matrix, (4) inverse, (5) transformed $\bar{A}$, $\bar{B}$, $\bar{C}$, (6) verification of at least one invariant. - ✅ **Stability tip:** If all $\lambda_i$ have negative real parts, the diagonal system is **asymptotically stable** — visible directly from the diagonal entries.

topics.title: Solution of Time Response of Autonomous Systems and Forced Systems, State Space Analysis of Discrete Time Control Systems: Phase Variable Form and Diagonal Canonical Form Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT635 - ADVANCED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solution of Time Response of Autonomous Systems and Forced Systems, State Space Analysis of Discrete Time Control Systems: Phase Variable Form and Diagonal Canonical Form Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Time Response of Autonomous Systems

An **autonomous (or homogeneous) system** is a state-space representation where the external input $u(t) = 0$ for all time. The system dynamics are governed entirely by the initial conditions and the inherent system matrix $A$.

The general continuous-time state equation for an LTI autonomous system is:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Autonomous = Self-driven.** The word "autonomous" comes from Greek *auto* (self) and *nomos* (law). An autonomous system obeys its own internal dynamics without any external forcing — like a pendulum swinging freely in a vacuum after being released.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a smooth ball placed on a perfectly frictionless bowl. Once released, the ball oscillates forever based purely on gravity and geometry — no one is pushing it. The "shape" of the bowl is the matrix $A$; the initial position $x_0$ determines the motion. This is the physical essence of an autonomous system.

### 1.2 Time Response of Forced (Non-Autonomous) Systems

A **forced system** receives an external input $u(t) \neq 0$. The complete continuous-time state equation becomes:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Take the same frictionless bowl, but now attach a small motor underneath that gently tilts the bowl left and right. The ball's motion is now the *superposition* of its natural oscillation (autonomous part) plus the forced response due to the tilting (particular part). This is exactly the structure of the **State Transition Matrix solution**.

### 1.3 Discrete-Time State Space Representation

A **discrete-time control system** models systems where signals are sampled at instants $kT$, giving a sequence of states:

$$x(k+1) = G\,x(k) + H\,u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k)$$

where $G$ is the **state matrix**, $H$ is the **input matrix**, $C$ is the **output matrix**, and $D$ is the **feedforward matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the **sampling period $T$** is the interval between consecutive samples, and the notation $x(k)$ represents the state vector at the $k^{th}$ sampling instant.

### 1.4 Phase Variable Form (Controllable Canonical Form)

The **Phase Variable Form** is a canonical state-space representation in which the state variables are successive integrals (or summations in discrete time) of the system input. The discrete-time transfer function:

$$\frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 z^n + b_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + b_n}{z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n}$$

is represented in phase variable form as:

$$x(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ -a_n & -a_{n-1} & -a_{n-2} & \cdots & -a_1 \end{bmatrix} x(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(k)$$

### 1.5 Diagonal Canonical Form (Modal Form)

The **Diagonal Canonical Form** decomposes the system into independent first-order modes by diagonalizing the $A$ (or $G$) matrix. Each state variable evolves independently.

$$x(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & \lambda_n \end{bmatrix} x(k) + \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ \vdots \\ b_n \end{bmatrix} u(k)$$

where $\lambda_i$ are the **eigenvalues** of $G$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$ are the **natural modes** of the system. A system is **stable** in discrete time if and only if $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ for all $i$ (i.e., all eigenvalues lie strictly inside the unit circle).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Solution of the Autonomous Continuous System

Starting with $\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t)$, applying the **Laplace Transform**:

$$sX(s) - x(0) = AX(s)$$

$$(sI - A)X(s) = x(0)$$

$$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}\,x(0)$$

Taking the inverse Laplace transform, the **State Transition Matrix** is defined as:

$$\phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$$

So the solution is:

$$\boxed{x(t) = e^{At}\,x(0) = \phi(t)\,x(0)}$$

**Properties of $e^{At}$:**

- $\phi(0) = e^{A\cdot 0} = I$ (identity matrix)
- $\phi(t_1 + t_2) = \phi(t_1)\,\phi(t_2)$ (semigroup property)
- $\phi^{-1}(t) = \phi(-t)$ (invertibility)
- $\frac{d}{dt}e^{At} = A\,e^{At} = e^{At}\,A$

**Methods of Computing $e^{At}$:**

1. **Laplace Transform method** — compute $(sI - A)^{-1}$ and invert.
2. **Cayley-Hamilton Theorem** — express $e^{At}$ as a polynomial in $A$ of degree $(n-1)$.
3. **Series Expansion** — $e^{At} = I + At + \frac{(At)^2}{2!} + \frac{(At)^3}{3!} + \cdots$
4. **Diagonalization / Jordan Form** — for distinct eigenvalues.

### 2.2 Solution of the Forced Continuous System

For $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ with $x(0) = x_0$, applying Laplace:

$$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}x(0) + (sI - A)^{-1}BU(s)$$

The complete time-domain solution is:

$$\boxed{x(t) = \underbrace{e^{At}x(0)}_{\text{Zero-input response}} + \underbrace{\int_0^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)}Bu(\tau)\,d\tau}_{\text{Zero-state response}}}$$

The output is:

$$y(t) = C\,x(t) + D\,u(t)$$

### 2.3 Solution of Discrete-Time Systems

#### 2.3.1 Autonomous Discrete System

For $x(k+1) = G\,x(k)$, iterating:

$$x(1) = G\,x(0)$$
$$x(2) = G\,x(1) = G^2\,x(0)$$
$$\boxed{x(k) = G^k\,x(0)}$$

The matrix $G^k$ is the **discrete state transition matrix** $\phi(k)$.

#### 2.3.2 Forced Discrete System

For $x(k+1) = G\,x(k) + H\,u(k)$:

$$x(1) = G\,x(0) + H\,u(0)$$
$$x(2) = G^2x(0) + GH\,u(0) + H\,u(1)$$
$$\vdots$$

The general solution is:

$$\boxed{x(k) = G^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} G^{k-1-j}\,H\,u(j)}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time |
|---|---|---|
| State Equation | $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ | $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$ |
| Autonomous Solution | $x(t) = e^{At}\,x(0)$ | $x(k) = G^k\,x(0)$ |
| Forced Solution | $x(t) = e^{At}x(0) + \int_0^t e^{A(t-\tau)}Bu(\tau)d\tau$ | $x(k) = G^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1}G^{k-1-j}Hu(j)$ |
| State Transition Matrix | $\phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}$ | $\phi(k) = G^k$ |
| Stability Condition | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all $i$ | $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ for all $i$ |
| Phase Variable $G$ Last Row | $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$ | $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$ |
| Phase Variable $H$ | $[0, 0, \ldots, 1]^T$ | $[0, 0, \ldots, 1]^T$ |
| Output Equation | $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$ | $y(k) = Cx(k) + Du(k)$ |
| Discretization (ZOH) | $G = e^{AT}$, $H = \int_0^T e^{A\tau}d\tau \cdot B$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The **last row** of the phase variable matrix contains the **negated coefficients** of the characteristic polynomial (in reverse order). The **last element** of the $H$ vector is always **1**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Phase Variable Form** is the starting point for **pole-placement controller design** because the controllability matrix becomes trivially canonical.
- **Diagonal Canonical Form** is essential for **modal analysis** — each eigenvalue represents an independent physical mode (e.g., vibrational modes of a structure, electrical resonances in a power system).
- **Discrete state transition matrices** are the workhorse of **digital filter design** and **Kalman filter prediction** in modern control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Solution of the Autonomous Continuous System

We begin with:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Laplace transform to both sides.**

Using the property $\mathcal{L}\{\dot{x}(t)\} = sX(s) - x(0)$:

$$sX(s) - x(0) = A\,X(s)$$

**Step 2 — Collect the $X(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.**

Subtract $AX(s)$ from both sides:

$$sX(s) - A\,X(s) = x(0)$$

Factor out $X(s)$:

$$(sI - A)\,X(s) = x(0)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $X(s)$.**

Pre-multiply by the inverse of $(sI - A)$ (which exists for $\det(sI - A) \neq 0$):

$$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}\,x(0)$$

**Step 4 — Take the inverse Laplace transform.**

Define the state transition matrix:

$$\phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$$

Therefore:

$$x(t) = e^{At}\,x(0) = \phi(t)\,x_0 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Solution of the Forced Continuous System

We begin with:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

**Step 1 — Laplace transform both sides.**

$$sX(s) - x(0) = A\,X(s) + B\,U(s)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange.**

$$(sI - A)X(s) = x(0) + B\,U(s)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $X(s)$.**

$$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1}x(0) + (sI - A)^{-1}B\,U(s)$$

**Step 4 — Apply convolution theorem.**

Recognizing that multiplication in the $s$-domain corresponds to convolution in the time domain:

$$x(t) = e^{At}x(0) + \int_0^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)}B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Forced Discrete-Time System Solution

We begin with:

$$x(k+1) = G\,x(k) + H\,u(k)$$

**Step 1 — Expand iteratively.**

For $k = 0$:

$$x(1) = G\,x(0) + H\,u(0)$$

For $k = 1$:

$$x(2) = G\,x(1) + H\,u(1) = G^2x(0) + GH\,u(0) + H\,u(1)$$

For $k = 2$:

$$x(3) = G^3x(0) + G^2H\,u(0) + GH\,u(1) + H\,u(2)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the pattern.**

In general, for step $k$:

$$x(k) = G^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} G^{k-1-j}\,H\,u(j) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> The summation index $j$ counts "how long ago" the input $u(j)$ was applied, weighted by the transition matrix $G^{k-1-j}$ which represents the propagation of that effect forward in time.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: State Transition Matrix via Laplace

**Problem:** Given $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, compute $e^{At}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $(sI - A)$.**

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the determinant.**

$$\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) + 2 = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the adjugate and inverse.**

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Partial fraction expansion.**

$$\frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{-2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-2}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{s}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-1}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}$$

**Step 5 — Take the inverse Laplace transform element-wise.**

$$e^{At} = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.5 Conversion: Transfer Function → Phase Variable Form (Discrete)

**Given Transfer Function:**

$$\frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 z^2 + b_1 z + b_2}{z^2 + a_1 z + a_2}$$

**Step 1 — Cross-multiply.**

$$(z^2 + a_1 z + a_2)Y(z) = (b_0 z^2 + b_1 z + b_2)U(z)$$

**Step 2 — Define phase variables.**

$$x_1(k) = y(k) - b_0 u(k)$$

$$x_2(k) = x_1(k+1) - b_1 u(k)$$

**Step 3 — Derive the state equations.**

$$x_1(k+1) = x_2(k) + b_1 u(k)$$

$$x_2(k+1) = -a_2 x_1(k) - a_1 x_2(k) + b_2 u(k)$$

**Step 4 — Write in matrix form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_1(k+1) \\ x_2(k+1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -a_2 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} u(k)$$

$$y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} + b_0 u(k)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the appearance of $b_1, b_2$ (not $b_0$) in $H$. This is because the **feedforward term $b_0$** moves to the $D$ matrix of the output equation.

---

### 3.6 Conversion: Transfer Function → Diagonal Canonical Form

**Given:** $G$ has distinct eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n$.

**Step 1 — Compute the modal matrix $M$ from eigenvectors.**

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} v_1 & v_2 & \cdots & v_n \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Diagonalize.**

$$G_d = M^{-1} G M = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n)$$

**Step 3 — Transform state vectors.**

$$x_d(k) = M^{-1} x(k)$$

**Step 4 — Write the diagonal form.**

$$x_d(k+1) = G_d\,x_d(k) + M^{-1} H\,u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C\,M\,x_d(k) + D\,u(k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **modal matrix $M$** is invertible only when the system has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors — this is guaranteed when $G$ has **distinct eigenvalues**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Forced State-Space System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r1[Reference Input u of k] --> sum1((Sum Node +))
    sum1 --> ssc1[State Space Controller: G and H matrices]
    ssc1 --> out1[State Vector x of k plus 1]
    ssc1 --> fb1[Feedback Path: C matrix]
    fb1 --> sum1
    ssc1 --> y1[Output y of k]
    out1 --> dly[DELAY: One Step z to the minus 1]
    dly --> ssc1
    y1 --> plant1[Plant Process Realization]
    plant1 --> y2[Physical Output Response]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the **closed-loop discrete-time control architecture** showing the role of the state transition matrix $G$, input matrix $H$, and the delayed feedback path.

### 4.2 Concept Map: State-Space Solution Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Continuous Time Domain: x dot equals A x plus B u] --> laplace1[Apply Laplace Transform]
    laplace1 --> inv1[Compute sI minus A Inverse]
    inv1 --> stm1[State Transition Matrix: phi of t equals e to the A t]
    stm1 --> sol1[Time Response: x of t equals phi t x0 plus convolution integral]
    
    startB[Discrete Time Domain: x of k plus 1 equals G x of k plus H u of k] --> iter1[Iterative Unrolling]
    iter1 --> stm2[State Transition Matrix: phi of k equals G to the power k]
    stm2 --> sol2[Time Response: x of k equals G to the k x0 plus summation]
    
    startC[Transfer Function: Y over U in z domain] --> branch1{Canonical Form Choice}
    branch1 --> pv1[Phase Variable Form: Companion Matrix]
    branch1 --> diag1[Diagonal Form: Eigenvalue Decomposition]
    
    sol1 --> ktu1[KTU Board Exam Output]
    sol2 --> ktu1
    pv1 --> ktu1
    diag1 --> ktu1
```

### 4.3 Comparison Architecture: Phase Variable vs Diagonal Form

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tf1[Transfer Function Y over U] --> cf1{Canonical Representation}
    cf1 --> pv2[PHASE VARIABLE FORM]
    cf1 --> dg2[DIAGONAL CANONICAL FORM]
    
    pv2 --> pv3[Coupled State Equations]
    pv2 --> pv4[Companion Matrix Structure]
    pv2 --> pv5[Controllability Index: Trivial]
    
    dg2 --> dg3[Decoupled State Equations]
    dg2 --> dg4[Diagonal Eigenvalue Matrix]
    dg2 --> dg5[Modal Independence Property]
    
    pv3 --> out2[Designed for Pole Placement]
    dg3 --> out3[Designed for Modal Analysis]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow: Discrete-Time Forced Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inpA[Initial State x of 0] --> path1
    inpB[Input Sequence u of j] --> path2
    path1 --> mul1[Multiply by G to the power k]
    path2 --> mul2[Multiply by G to the power k minus 1 minus j]
    path2 --> mul3[Multiply by H matrix]
    mul1 --> adder((Summer))
    mul2 --> mul3
    mul3 --> adder
    adder --> outF[Total Response x of k]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **signal flow graph** clearly shows the two-part structure of the forced response: the **zero-input** (autonomous) component and the **zero-state** (forced) component, summed together to give the total state.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define an autonomous system. Write the state equation and its solution.*

**Model Answer:**
An **autonomous system** is a system whose dynamics are governed entirely by its initial conditions, with no external input applied ($u(t) = 0$).

State equation:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t), \quad x(0) = x_0$$

Solution:

$$x(t) = e^{At}\,x(0) = \phi(t)\,x_0$$

where $\phi(t) = e^{At}$ is the state transition matrix.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the stability condition for a discrete-time system in terms of its eigenvalues.*

**Model Answer:**
A discrete-time system $x(k+1) = Gx(k)$ is **asymptotically stable** if and only if all eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $G$ satisfy:

$$\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

That is, all eigenvalues must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** in the complex $z$-plane.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** For the system $\dot{x}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{bmatrix} x(t) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(t)$ with $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and $u(t) = 1$ (unit step), determine the complete state response $x(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find the state transition matrix using Laplace.**

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 6 & s+5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(sI - A) = s(s+5) + 6 = s^2 + 5s + 6 = (s+2)(s+3)$$

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} \begin{bmatrix} s+5 & 1 \\ -6 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Partial fractions.**

$$\frac{s+5}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{3}{s+2} - \frac{2}{s+3}$$

$$\frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{1}{s+3}$$

$$\frac{-6}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{-6}{s+2} + \frac{6}{s+3}$$

$$\frac{s}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{-2}{s+2} + \frac{3}{s+3}$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Laplace.**

$$e^{At} = \begin{bmatrix} 3e^{-2t} - 2e^{-3t} & e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} \\ -6e^{-2t} + 6e^{-3t} & -2e^{-2t} + 3e^{-3t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Zero-input response.**

$$x_{zi}(t) = e^{At}\,x(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 3e^{-2t} - 2e^{-3t} \\ -6e^{-2t} + 6e^{-3t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Zero-state response with $U(s) = 1/s$.**

$$X_{zs}(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} B \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{s(s+2)(s+3)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}$$

For first element: $\frac{1}{s(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{1/6}{s} - \frac{1/2}{s+2} + \frac{1/3}{s+3}$

For second element: $\frac{s}{s(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{s+2} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{s+3}$ — wait, recompute:

$\frac{1}{s(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{1/6}{s} + \frac{-1/2}{s+2} + \frac{1/3}{s+3}$

So the second element numerator is $s$:

$\frac{s}{s(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{1}{s+3}$ → wait, recompute properly:

$\frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{1}{s+3}$, so $X_{zs,1}(t) = \frac{1}{6} - \frac{1}{2}e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{3}e^{-3t}$

$X_{zs,2}(t) = e^{-2t} - e^{-3t}$

**Step 6 — Total response.**

$$x_1(t) = 3e^{-2t} - 2e^{-3t} + \frac{1}{6} - \frac{1}{2}e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{3}e^{-3t} = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{5}{2}e^{-2t} - \frac{5}{3}e^{-3t}$$

$$x_2(t) = -6e^{-2t} + 6e^{-3t} + e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} = -5e^{-2t} + 5e^{-3t}$$

---

**(b)** Convert the discrete transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{z+2}{z^2 + 3z + 2}$ into phase variable canonical form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients.**

$$a_1 = 3, \quad a_2 = 2, \quad b_0 = 0, \quad b_1 = 1, \quad b_2 = 2$$

**Step 2 — Construct the phase variable state matrix $G_{pv}$.**

$$G_{pv} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -a_2 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the input matrix $H_{pv}$.**

$$H_{pv} = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Construct the output matrix $C_{pv}$.**

$$C_{pv} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Direct feedthrough $D = b_0 = 0$.**

**Final Phase Variable Form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} x_1(k+1) \\ x_2(k+1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} u(k)$$

$$y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the complete solution of the forced continuous-time state equation $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ with initial condition $x(0) = x_0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the Laplace transform.**

$$sX(s) - x_0 = AX(s) + BU(s)$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange.**

$$(sI - A)X(s) = x_0 + BU(s)$$

**Step 3 — Pre-multiply by $(sI - A)^{-1}$.**

$$X(s) = (sI - A)^{-1} x_0 + (sI - A)^{-1} B\, U(s)$$

**Step 4 — Define state transition matrix.**

$$\phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$$

**Step 5 — Apply inverse Laplace transform with the convolution theorem.**

$$\boxed{x(t) = e^{At} x_0 + \int_0^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau)\, d\tau}$$

where the first term is the **zero-input response** and the second term is the **zero-state response**.

---

**(b)** For the discrete system $G = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = -1$ and $\lambda_2 = -2$, find the corresponding eigenvectors and write the system in diagonal canonical form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find eigenvector for $\lambda_1 = -1$.**

$$(G - \lambda_1 I)v_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix} v_1 = 0$$

Row reduces to $v_{1,1} + v_{1,2} = 0$, so $v_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2 — Find eigenvector for $\lambda_2 = -2$.**

$$(G - \lambda_2 I)v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} v_2 = 0$$

Row reduces to $2v_{2,1} + v_{2,2} = 0$, so $v_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 3 — Form the modal matrix $M$.**

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad M^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the diagonal form.**

$$G_d = M^{-1} G M = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — If $H = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the transformed $H_d$.**

$$H_d = M^{-1} H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Diagonal Canonical Form:**

$$x_d(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} x_d(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} u(k)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign errors in the last row** of the phase variable matrix: Students often write $[a_n, a_{n-1}, \ldots, a_1]$ instead of $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$. **Always include the negative sign.**
> 2. **Confusing $b_0$ with $b_1$ in $H$:** $b_0$ belongs to the $D$ matrix (feedthrough), not the $H$ matrix. This is a **very common 1-mark loss**.
> 3. **Forgetting the convolution integral** in the forced response: KTU specifically asks for both the zero-input and zero-state parts. Skipping one loses 3-4 marks.
> 4. **Stability criterion confusion:** Students write continuous-time stability ($\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$) for discrete systems. **Always use $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$ for discrete systems.**
> 5. **Modal matrix invertibility:** Students forget to verify that eigenvectors are linearly independent. Always check that $\det(M) \neq 0$ before forming the diagonal form.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Autonomous system** = $u(t) = 0$; solution is $x(t) = e^{At}x(0)$.
- **Forced system** = zero-input response $+$ zero-state response (convolution integral).
- **Discrete forced solution** = $G^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} G^{k-1-j} H u(j)$.
- **State transition matrix** = $\phi(t) = e^{At}$ in continuous time; $\phi(k) = G^k$ in discrete time.
- **Phase variable form (Controllable Canonical):** Last row is $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$; $H$ ends with $1$ (or $b_n$).
- **Diagonal canonical form:** Uses eigenvalues on the diagonal; requires $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors.
- **Continuous stability:** $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ (left half plane).
- **Discrete stability:** $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle).
- **Output equation:** $y = Cx + Du$; $D = b_0$ for systems with feedthrough.
- **ZOH discretization:** $G = e^{AT}$, $H = \int_0^T e^{A\tau} B\, d\tau$.
- **Always check** $\det(M) \neq 0$ before performing similarity transformation for diagonal form.
- **Pole-placement design** uses phase variable form; **modal analysis** uses diagonal form.
- **KTU marking pattern:** State transition matrix derivation carries 4-5 marks, full state response carries 7-8 marks, canonical form conversion carries 6-7 marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Exam Hack:** When asked for $e^{At}$, the fastest way is to compute $(sI - A)^{-1}$ using a single matrix inverse formula for $2 \times 2$ matrices, then partial-fraction. For $3 \times 3$, use **Cayley-Hamilton** with $e^{At} = \alpha_0 I + \alpha_1 A + \alpha_2 A^2$ where $\alpha_i$ are solved from $e^{\lambda_i t} = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \lambda_i + \alpha_2 \lambda_i^2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Transfer Function from State Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT635 - ADVANCED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pulse Transfer Function from State Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Transfer Function from State Matrix:**
> The **Pulse Transfer Function (PTF)** is the Z-domain input–output ratio of a discrete-time (sampled-data) linear time-invariant (LTI) system. When the system is described by a **continuous-time state-space model**, the state matrix **A**, input matrix **B**, output matrix **C**, and direct transmission matrix **D** must first be discretized over the sampling interval **T** to obtain the discrete state matrices **G(T)** and **H(T)**. The pulse transfer function is then extracted by Z-transforming the discrete state equations and eliminating the state vector.

Mathematically, given a continuous LTI system:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t)$$

$$y(t) = C\,x(t) + D\,u(t)$$

The equivalent **discrete-time state model** under a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) and sampling period **T** is:

$$x[(k+1)T] = G(T)\,x(kT) + H(T)\,u(kT)$$

$$y(kT) = C\,x(kT) + D\,u(kT)$$

where **G(T)** is the **state transition matrix** and **H(T)** is the **discrete input coupling matrix**. The pulse transfer function is then obtained as:

$$\boxed{G_p(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H + D}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Elevator Analogy:**
> Imagine a continuous elevator moving smoothly between floors. To *digitally* control it with a microcontroller that only acts every 1 second, we cannot command "move continuously." We must ask, "If I press Floor 5 right now, where exactly will the elevator be 1 second later, and where will it be 2 seconds later?"
>
> - The continuous state matrix **A** is like the elevator's *physics equations of motion*.
> - The discrete state matrix **G(T)** answers the question: *"Where will the state be one sampling interval later, given the current state?"* — it is essentially **e^(AT)**, the *time-propagation operator*.
> - The matrix **H(T)** answers: *"If I apply a constant input (button press) for the whole 1-second interval, what total state change does it produce?"*
> - Once we have **G** and **H**, the Z-transform acts like a *translator* that converts time-recursive elevator positions into a polynomial ratio of outputs to inputs — and that ratio is the **Pulse Transfer Function**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling period | **T** | **seconds (s)** | Time between two successive samples; must satisfy **Shannon-Nyquist criterion** |
| State transition matrix | **G(T)** | **dimensionless** | Propagates the state vector across one sampling interval |
| Discrete input matrix | **H(T)** | varies with input | Integral of input effect over one sampling interval |
| State matrix (continuous) | **A** | **s⁻¹** (typically) | Continuous-time dynamics |
| Output matrix | **C** | varies | Maps state to output |
| Identity matrix | **I** | **dimensionless** | **n × n** unit matrix for an n-th order system |

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Discretization Core:**
> Pulse Transfer Function from State Matrix forms the *bridge* between continuous-time classical control (state-space) and discrete-time digital control. Every digital controller implementation — from Arduino-based motor drives to industrial PLCs — uses this exact transformation. KTU examiners frequently test this as a **Part B (14-mark)** problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — From Continuous State to Pulse Transfer Function

The conversion pipeline consists of **three sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Discretization of Continuous State Equations**
The solution to $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ is given by the **variation of parameters** formula:

$$x(t) = e^{A(t-t_0)}\,x(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)}\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau$$

Applying ZOH (u(τ) constant = u(kT) for kT ≤ τ < (k+1)T) and evaluating at t = (k+1)T with t₀ = kT:

$$x[(k+1)T] = \underbrace{e^{AT}}_{G(T)}\,x(kT) + \underbrace{\left[\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\lambda}\,B\,d\lambda\right]}_{H(T)}\,u(kT)$$

**Stage 2 — Z-Transform of the Discrete State Equation**
Taking the one-sided Z-transform of $x[(k+1)T] = G\,x(kT) + H\,u(kT)$:

$$z\,X(z) - z\,x(0) = G\,X(z) + H\,U(z)$$

Assuming zero initial conditions (x(0) = 0, standard for transfer functions):

$$X(z) = (zI - G)^{-1}\,H\,U(z)$$

**Stage 3 — Output Equation Elimination**
The output equation $y(kT) = C\,x(kT) + D\,u(kT)$ transforms to:

$$Y(z) = C\,X(z) + D\,U(z) = \left[C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H + D\right]U(z)$$

Therefore, the pulse transfer function is:

$$\boxed{G_p(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H + D}$$

### 2.2 Why & How Behind Each Step

- **Why e^(AT)?** — The matrix exponential is the continuous-time state propagator. Just as $x(t) = e^{at}x(0)$ in scalar ODEs, in matrix form $x(t) = e^{At}x(0)$ when u = 0.
- **Why ZOH assumption?** — Digital-to-Analog converters physically hold the constant value between samples, justifying the piecewise-constant input.
- **Why (zI − G)⁻¹?** — Algebraic rearrangement to solve the linear difference equation for X(z) in terms of U(z).
- **Why initial conditions = 0?** — The transfer function is defined purely as the input–output ratio under zero state, isolating the system's intrinsic dynamics.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Compilation** — Master these for guaranteed marks.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Dimension |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G(T) = e^{AT}$ | Discrete state (propagation) matrix | dimensionless |
| 2 | $H(T) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\lambda}\,B\,d\lambda$ | Discrete input coupling matrix | input-scaled |
| 3 | $G_p(z) = C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H + D$ | Pulse Transfer Function from state | ratio |
| 4 | $G(T) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}\big\vert_{t=T}$ | Laplace-based computation of G | dimensionless |
| 5 | $G(T) = I + AT + \dfrac{(AT)^2}{2!} + \dfrac{(AT)^3}{3!} + \cdots$ | Series expansion of e^(AT) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $G_p(z) = \dfrac{\text{adj}(zI - G)}{\det(zI - G)}\cdot H$ | Cramer's-rule expansion | ratio |
| 7 | $\det(zI - G) = 0$ | Characteristic equation of discrete system | polynomial = 0 |
| 8 | $\det(zI - G) = z^n - \alpha_1 z^{n-1} - \cdots - \alpha_n$ | Standard polynomial form (KTU form) | polynomial |
| 9 | Stability condition | All roots of $\det(zI - G) = 0$ must lie **inside unit circle** $\vert z\vert < 1$ | — |
| 10 | Relation to continuous poles $s_i$ | Discrete poles: $z_i = e^{s_i T}$ | complex mapping |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where this is applied in Industry:**
>
> - **Digital Motor Controllers (BLDC, PMSM, Servo drives):** Inner current loop implemented digitally; state matrices discretized for real-time computation on DSPs/FPGAs.
> - **Aircraft Autopilot Systems:** Discrete pitch/yaw state matrices updated every 10–50 ms; pulse transfer function used for stability analysis.
> - **Robotic Manipulator Control:** Joint-angle state vectors discretized; PTF used for trajectory tracking.
> - **Process Control (Petrochemical, Power Plants):** Sampled-data DCS systems; PTF derived from state matrices of MIMO processes.
> - **Automotive ECU (Engine Control Units):** Crankshaft angular state discretized; fuel injection pulse-transfer modeling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of G(T) and H(T) from the Continuous State Equation

**Given:** Continuous LTI system

$$\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t)$$

**Step 1:** Write the homogeneous (zero-input) solution using the variation-of-parameters formula.

$$x(t) = e^{At}\,x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)}\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the ZOH input assumption: for $kT \le t < (k+1)T$, $u(t) = u(kT)$ = constant.

**Step 3:** Evaluate $x(t)$ at $t = (k+1)T$ and at $t = kT$.

At $t = (k+1)T$:

$$x[(k+1)T] = e^{A(k+1)T}\,x(0) + \int_{0}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau$$

At $t = kT$:

$$x(kT) = e^{AkT}\,x(0) + \int_{0}^{kT} e^{A(kT - \tau)}\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 4:** Multiply the second equation by $e^{AT}$:

$$e^{AT}\,x(kT) = e^{A(k+1)T}\,x(0) + \int_{0}^{kT} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Subtract the second (multiplied) equation from the first:

$$x[(k+1)T] - e^{AT}\,x(kT) = \int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 6:** Use the ZOH fact that $u(\tau) = u(kT)$ over the interval $[kT,\,(k+1)T]$ and pull it out of the integral:

$$x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}\,x(kT) + \left[\int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,B\,d\tau\right] u(kT)$$

**Step 7:** Change variable $\lambda = (k+1)T - \tau$ so $d\lambda = -d\tau$. When $\tau = kT$, $\lambda = T$; when $\tau = (k+1)T$, $\lambda = 0$:

$$\int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,B\,d\tau = \int_{T}^{0} e^{A\lambda}\,B\,(-d\lambda) = \int_{0}^{T} e^{A\lambda}\,B\,d\lambda$$

**Step 8:** Therefore, identifying $G(T) = e^{AT}$ and $H(T) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\lambda}\,B\,d\lambda$:

$$\boxed{x[(k+1)T] = G(T)\,x(kT) + H(T)\,u(kT)}$$

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Pulse Transfer Function

**Given:** Discrete state equations

$$x[(k+1)T] = G\,x(kT) + H\,u(kT)$$

$$y(kT) = C\,x(kT) + D\,u(kT)$$

**Step 1:** Apply the one-sided Z-transform to the state equation. Recall that $\mathcal{Z}\{x(kT)\} = X(z)$ and $\mathcal{Z}\{x[(k+1)T]\} = z\,X(z) - z\,x(0)$.

Setting initial condition $x(0) = 0$:

$$z\,X(z) = G\,X(z) + H\,U(z)$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $X(z)$:

$$z\,X(z) - G\,X(z) = H\,U(z)$$

$$(zI - G)\,X(z) = H\,U(z)$$

$$X(z) = (zI - G)^{-1}\,H\,U(z)$$

**Step 3:** Apply Z-transform to the output equation:

$$Y(z) = C\,X(z) + D\,U(z)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $X(z)$:

$$Y(z) = C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H\,U(z) + D\,U(z)$$

**Step 5:** Factor $U(z)$:

$$Y(z) = \left[C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H + D\right] U(z)$$

**Step 6:** Identify the pulse transfer function as the transfer-function matrix:

$$\boxed{G_p(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H + D}$$

### 3.3 Method 1 — Computation of G(T) via Laplace Transform

This is the most commonly tested method in KTU examinations.

**Step 1:** Form the resolvent matrix $(sI - A)$.

**Step 2:** Compute its inverse:

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{\text{adj}(sI - A)}{\det(sI - A)}$$

**Step 3:** Take the inverse Laplace transform of every entry:

$$G(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at $t = T$ to obtain $G(T)$.

### 3.4 Method 2 — Computation of H(T) by Direct Integration

$$H(T) = \int_{0}^{T} G(\lambda)\,B\,d\lambda$$

Or, using the Laplace relation:

$$H(T) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{(sI - A)^{-1}\,B}{s}\right\}\Bigg\vert_{t=T}$$

### 3.5 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement:** A continuous-time LTI system has:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

Find the pulse transfer function $G_p(z)$ for a sampling period **T = 1 second**.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute G(T) using Laplace Method

Form $sI - A$:

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 0 & s + 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute determinant and adjugate:

$$\det(sI - A) = s(s + 2)$$

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s + 2 & 1 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute inverse:

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s + 2 & 1 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{s} & \dfrac{1}{s(s+2)} \\[2mm] 0 & \dfrac{1}{s+2} \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply partial fractions:

$$\frac{1}{s(s+2)} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s+2}\right)$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$G(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2t}) \\[2mm] 0 & e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

Evaluate at **t = T = 1**:

$$G(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2}) \\[2mm] 0 & e^{-2} \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.4323 \\ 0 & 0.1353 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute H(T) by Direct Integration

$$H(T) = \int_{0}^{1} G(\lambda)\,B\,d\lambda = \int_{0}^{1} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2\lambda}) \\[2mm] e^{-2\lambda} \end{bmatrix} d\lambda$$

Component-wise integration:

$$H_1(1) = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2\lambda})\,d\lambda = \frac{1}{2}\left[\lambda + \frac{e^{-2\lambda}}{2}\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(1 + \frac{e^{-2}}{2}\right) - \left(0 + \frac{1}{2}\right)\right]$$

$$H_1(1) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2} + \frac{e^{-2}}{2}\right] = \frac{1}{4}(1 + e^{-2}) \approx \frac{1}{4}(1.1353) \approx 0.2838$$

$$H_2(1) = \int_{0}^{1} e^{-2\lambda}\,d\lambda = \left[-\frac{e^{-2\lambda}}{2}\right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2}) \approx 0.4323$$

Therefore:

$$H(1) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.2838 \\ 0.4323 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3 — Form $zI - G$ and its Inverse

$$zI - G = \begin{bmatrix} z - 1 & -0.4323 \\ 0 & z - 0.1353 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(zI - G) = (z - 1)(z - 0.1353)$$

$$(zI - G)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(z-1)(z-0.1353)} \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.1353 & 0.4323 \\ 0 & z - 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute $C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H$

$$(zI - G)^{-1}\,H = \frac{1}{(z-1)(z-0.1353)} \begin{bmatrix} 0.2838(z-0.1353) + 0.4323(0.4323) \\ 0.4323(z - 1) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \frac{1}{(z-1)(z-0.1353)} \begin{bmatrix} 0.2838\,z - 0.0384 + 0.1869 \\ 0.4323\,z - 0.4323 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{(z-1)(z-0.1353)} \begin{bmatrix} 0.2838\,z + 0.1485 \\ 0.4323\,z - 0.4323 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying by $C = [1, \ 0]$:

$$C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H = \frac{0.2838\,z + 0.1485}{(z-1)(z-0.1353)}$$

#### Step 5 — Final Pulse Transfer Function

Since $D = 0$:

$$\boxed{G_p(z) = \frac{0.2838\,z + 0.1485}{(z - 1)(z - 0.1353)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check for Stability:** Poles are at $z = 1$ (on the unit circle) and $z = 0.1353$ (inside). The system is **marginally unstable** due to the integrator pole — consistent with the original continuous system having a pole at $s = 0$.

---

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pulse Transfer Function Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Continuous State Space Model dot x = A x + B u and y = C x + D u"] --> nodeB["Apply Zero Order Hold ZOH and sampling period T"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compute State Transition Matrix G T equals e to power AT"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Compute Discrete Input Matrix H T equals integral 0 to T e to power A lambda B d lambda"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Form Discrete State Model x k+1 T equals G x kT plus H u kT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["Apply Z Transform to discrete state equation with zero initial conditions"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Solve for X z in terms of U z: X z equals zI minus G inverse H U z"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Apply Z Transform to output equation: Y z equals C X z plus D U z"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Substitute X z expression into Y z"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Final Pulse Transfer Function: Gp z equals C zI minus G inverse H plus D"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Stability Check: All poles of det zI minus G must satisfy mod z less than 1"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Laplace Method Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Given Matrix A"] --> node2["Form sI minus A"]
    node2 --> node3["Compute Determinant det sI minus A"]
    node2 --> node4["Compute Adjugate adj sI minus A"]
    node3 --> node5["Inverse Matrix formula adj divided by det"]
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6["Apply Partial Fraction Decomposition"]
    node6 --> node7["Inverse Laplace Transform each entry"]
    node7 --> node8["Evaluate at t equals T to obtain G T"]
    node1 --> node9["Given Matrix B"]
    node8 --> node10["Integrate G lambda B from 0 to T"]
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11["Compute H T"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship Between Continuous and Discrete Poles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cp["Continuous Pole s sub i"] --> map["Mapping Rule: z sub i equals e to power s sub i T"]
    map --> dp["Discrete Pole z sub i"]
    dp --> stableCheck{"mod z sub i less than 1"}
    stableCheck -- YES --> stableText["Discrete system is STABLE"]
    stableCheck -- NO --> unstableText["Discrete system is UNSTABLE"]
    cp --> lhp{"Re s sub i less than 0"}
    lhp -- YES --> contStable["Continuous system is STABLE"]
    lhp -- NO --> contUnstable["Continuous system is UNSTABLE"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Sampled-Data State Feedback Control Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rkT["Reference r kT"] --> sumJunction(("Sum: plus minus"))
    sumJunction --> ukT["Control Input u kT"]
    ukT --> zoh["Zero Order Hold ZOH"]
    zoh --> ut["Continuous u t"]
    ut --> plantA["Continuous Plant A B C D"]
    plantA --> yt["Continuous Output y t"]
    yt --> sampler["Sampler period T"]
    sampler --> ykT["Sampled Output y kT"]
    ykT --> sumJunction
    plantA --> stateVec["Continuous State x t"]
    stateVec --> stateSampler["State Sampler period T"]
    stateSampler --> xkT["Sampled State x kT"]
    xkT --> gainK["State Feedback Gain Matrix K"]
    gainK --> fbSignal["Feedback Signal K times x kT"]
    fbSignal --> sumJunction
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Pulse Transfer Function in the context of a discrete-time system described by a state-space model. List the discrete state matrices required to compute it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Pulse Transfer Function (PTF)** of a discrete-time LTI system is the ratio of the Z-transform of the output sequence $y(kT)$ to the Z-transform of the input sequence $u(kT)$ under zero initial conditions.

For a discrete system represented in state-space form:

$$x[(k+1)T] = G(T)\,x(kT) + H(T)\,u(kT)$$

$$y(kT) = C\,x(kT) + D\,u(kT)$$

the PTF is given by:

$$G_p(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = C\,(zI - G)^{-1}\,H + D$$

The required discrete state matrices are:
1. **G(T)** — discrete state (state transition) matrix
2. **H(T)** — discrete input coupling matrix
3. **C** — output matrix (carried over from continuous system)
4. **D** — direct transmission matrix (carried over from continuous system)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the formula for the discrete state matrix G(T) and explain its physical meaning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The discrete state matrix is given by:

$$G(T) = e^{AT} = I + AT + \frac{(AT)^2}{2!} + \frac{(AT)^3}{3!} + \cdots$$

**Physical Meaning:** $G(T)$ propagates the system's state vector from sample instant $kT$ to the next sample instant $(k+1)T$ under zero-input conditions. It is the matrix analog of the scalar exponential $e^{at}$ which propagates a first-order state across time. When the input is absent, $x[(k+1)T] = G(T)\,x(kT)$, meaning $G(T)$ fully determines how the free (homogeneous) response evolves across sampling intervals.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**For a continuous-time LTI system with state-space matrices:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the discrete state matrices G(T) and H(T) for a sampling period of T = 0.5 seconds using the Laplace transform method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Hence obtain the pulse transfer function G_p(z) for the discretized system and comment on its stability.

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1:** Compute $sI - A$:

$$sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s + 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Determinant and adjugate:

$$\det(sI - A) = s(s + 3) + 2 = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

$$\text{adj}(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s + 3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Inverse:

$$(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s + 3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Partial fraction decomposition of each entry:

$$\frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{-2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-2}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}$$

$$\frac{s}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-1}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}$$

**Step 5:** Inverse Laplace transform:

$$G(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate at $T = 0.5$ s:

$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$, $e^{-1} = 0.3679$

$$G(0.5) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0.6065) - 0.3679 & 0.6065 - 0.3679 \\ -2(0.6065) + 2(0.3679) & -0.6065 + 2(0.3679) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8451 & 0.2386 \\ -0.4773 & 0.1292 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7:** Compute $H(0.5)$:

$$H(0.5) = \int_{0}^{0.5} G(\tau)\,B\,d\tau = \int_{0}^{0.5} \begin{bmatrix} e^{-\tau} - e^{-2\tau} \\ -e^{-\tau} + 2e^{-2\tau} \end{bmatrix} d\tau$$

$$H_1(0.5) = \left[-e^{-\tau} + \frac{e^{-2\tau}}{2}\right]_0^{0.5} = \left(-0.6065 + 0.1839\right) - \left(-1 + 0.5\right) = -0.4226 + 0.5 = 0.0774$$

$$H_2(0.5) = \left[e^{-\tau} - e^{-2\tau}\right]_0^{0.5} = \left(0.6065 - 0.3679\right) - \left(1 - 1\right) = 0.2386$$

$$H(0.5) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0774 \\ 0.2386 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1:** Form $zI - G$:

$$zI - G = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.8451 & -0.2386 \\ 0.4773 & z - 0.1292 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Determinant:

$$\det(zI - G) = (z - 0.8451)(z - 0.1292) - (-0.2386)(0.4773)$$

$$= z^2 - 0.9743z + 0.1092 + 0.1139 = z^2 - 0.9743z + 0.2231$$

**Step 3:** Adj$(zI - G)$:

$$\text{adj}(zI - G) = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.1292 & 0.2386 \\ -0.4773 & z - 0.8451 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator (ignoring $D = 0$):

$$N(z) = C \cdot \text{adj}(zI - G) \cdot H = [1, \ 1] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.1292 & 0.2386 \\ -0.4773 & z - 0.8451 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.0774 \\ 0.2386 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= [1, \ 1] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.0774(z - 0.1292) + 0.2386(0.2386) \\ -0.4773(0.0774) + 0.2386(z - 0.8451) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= [1, \ 1] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.0774z - 0.0100 + 0.0569 \\ -0.0369 + 0.2386z - 0.2016 \end{bmatrix} = [1, \ 1] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.0774z + 0.0469 \\ 0.2386z - 0.2385 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$N(z) = (0.0774z + 0.0469) + (0.2386z - 0.2385) = 0.3160z - 0.1916$$

**Step 5:** Pulse Transfer Function:

$$\boxed{G_p(z) = \frac{0.3160z - 0.1916}{z^2 - 0.9743z + 0.2231}}$$

**Step 6:** Stability comment — find discrete poles from $z^2 - 0.9743z + 0.2231 = 0$:

$$z = \frac{0.9743 \pm \sqrt{0.9493 - 0.8924}}{2} = \frac{0.9743 \pm \sqrt{0.0569}}{2} = \frac{0.9743 \pm 0.2386}{2}$$

$$z_1 \approx 0.6065, \quad z_2 \approx 0.3679$$

Both poles satisfy $\vert z \vert < 1$, so the discrete system is **STABLE**.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Consider the continuous-time state-space system:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the discrete state matrices G(T) and H(T) for T = 0.2 s using the series expansion method truncated to 4 terms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the pulse transfer function $G_p(z)$ and verify the stability by mapping continuous poles to discrete poles.

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Step 1:** Compute $AT$:

$$AT = \begin{bmatrix} -0.2 & 0 \\ 0.2 & -0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute successive powers:

$$(AT)^2 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.2 & 0 \\ 0.2 & -0.4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.2 & 0 \\ 0.2 & -0.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0 \\ -0.04 + (-0.08) & 0.16 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \text{(recheck)}$$

Recomputing $(AT)^2$:

Row 1: $(-0.2)(-0.2) + (0)(0.2) = 0.04$,  $(-0.2)(0) + (0)(-0.4) = 0$
Row 2: $(0.2)(-0.2) + (-0.4)(0.2) = -0.04 - 0.08 = -0.12$, $(0.2)(0) + (-0.4)(-0.4) = 0.16$

$$(AT)^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0 \\ -0.12 & 0.16 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$(AT)^3 = (AT)^2 (AT) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0 \\ -0.12 & 0.16 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -0.2 & 0 \\ 0.2 & -0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Row 1: $(0.04)(-0.2) + (0)(0.2) = -0.008$, $(0.04)(0) + (0)(-0.4) = 0$
Row 2: $(-0.12)(-0.2) + (0.16)(0.2) = 0.024 + 0.032 = 0.056$, $(-0.12)(0) + (0.16)(-0.4) = -0.064$

$$(AT)^3 = \begin{bmatrix} -0.008 & 0 \\ 0.056 & -0.064 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Series expansion (4 terms):

$$G(T) \approx I + AT + \frac{(AT)^2}{2!} + \frac{(AT)^3}{3!}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -0.2 & 0 \\ 0.2 & -0.4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.02 & 0 \\ -0.06 & 0.08 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -0.00133 & 0 \\ 0.00933 & -0.01067 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$G(0.2) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.8187 & 0 \\ 0.1493 & 0.6693 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $H(T) = \int_0^T G(\lambda) B\,d\lambda$ using $B = [1, 0]^T$. The first column of $G(t)$ for general $t$:

From eigenvalue analysis (eigenvalues of $A$ are $-1, -2$):

$$G(t) = \begin{bmatrix} e^{-t} & 0 \\ e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}$$

(This is exact; series above converges to this.)

**Step 5:** At $t = 0.2$, $e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$, $e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703$:

$$G(0.2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8187 & 0 \\ 0.1484 & 0.6703 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** First column integration:

$$H_1 = \int_0^{0.2} e^{-\tau} d\tau = 1 - e^{-0.2} = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813$$

$$H_2 = \int_0^{0.2} (e^{-\tau} - e^{-2\tau}) d\tau = \left[-e^{-\tau} + \frac{e^{-2\tau}}{2}\right]_0^{0.2}$$

$$= \left(-0.8187 + 0.3352\right) - \left(-1 + 0.5\right) = -0.4835 + 0.5 = 0.0165$$

$$H(0.2) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.1813 \\ 0.0165 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1:** Form $zI - G$:

$$zI - G = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.8187 & 0 \\ -0.1484 & z - 0.6703 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Determinant:

$$\det(zI - G) = (z - 0.8187)(z - 0.6703) = z^2 - 1.4890z + 0.5488$$

**Step 3:** Adj$(zI - G)$ and numerator:

$$\text{adj}(zI - G) \cdot H = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.6703 & 0 \\ 0.1484 & z - 0.8187 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.1813 \\ 0.0165 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1813(z - 0.6703) \\ 0.0269 + 0.0165(z - 0.8187) \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying by $C = [0, \ 1]$:

$$N(z) = 0.0269 + 0.0165z - 0.0135 = 0.0165z + 0.0134$$

**Step 4:** Pulse Transfer Function:

$$\boxed{G_p(z) = \frac{0.0165z + 0.0134}{z^2 - 1.4890z + 0.5488}}$$

**Step 5:** Stability verification via pole mapping:

Continuous poles: eigenvalues of $A$ are $s_1 = -1$ and $s_2 = -2$.

Mapped discrete poles: $z_i = e^{s_i T}$

$$z_1 = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187 \quad \checkmark$$
$$z_2 = e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703 \quad \checkmark$$

Both poles lie **inside the unit circle** ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$), confirming **STABILITY** of the discrete system.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Examiner Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting ZOH assumption** — When asked for the discrete state matrix, students sometimes write $G(T) = I + AT$ (Euler's approximation) instead of $G(T) = e^{AT}$. This is **incorrect** unless specifically asked for the forward-Euler approximation. Always use the exact exponential or Laplace inverse method. **(−3 marks typical deduction)**
>
> 2. **Sign error in adjugate** — When computing $(sI - A)^{-1}$ or $(zI - G)^{-1}$, students confuse the adjugate transpose with cofactor. Remember: $\text{adj}(M) = [\text{cofactor matrix}]^T$. A sign flip on one entry propagates everywhere. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Not setting initial conditions to zero** — The transfer function is defined ONLY for zero initial state. If you keep $x(0)$ in the derivation, you will get a wrong answer. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Incorrect integration limits** — For $H(T)$, the limits are $0$ to $T$, NOT $0$ to $\infty$. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Confusing G(T) with $\Phi(t)$** — While both equal $e^{At}$, G(T) is evaluated only at sample times and is a *constant* matrix in the discrete-time model. Students often write time-varying expressions where constants are needed. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Skipping stability comment** — In part (b), always comment on whether poles are inside the unit circle. KTU examiners allot 1–2 marks specifically for the stability analysis. **(−1 to −2 marks)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pulse Transfer Function from State Matrix**
>
> ✅ The Pulse Transfer Function bridges continuous state-space and discrete input-output models.
>
> ✅ **Three-Stage Pipeline:** Discretize (G, H) → Z-transform → Eliminate state vector.
>
> ✅ **Key formulas (must memorize):**
> - $G(T) = e^{AT}$
> - $H(T) = \int_{0}^{T} e^{A\lambda} B\,d\lambda$
> - $G_p(z) = C(zI - G)^{-1} H + D$
>
> ✅ **Two methods to compute G(T):**
> - **Laplace method:** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI-A)^{-1}\}\big\vert_{t=T}$ — most accurate, KTU-preferred.
> - **Series expansion:** $I + AT + \frac{(AT)^2}{2!} + \cdots$ — converges for small T.
> - **Cayley-Hamilton method:** Using characteristic polynomial of A — most efficient for high-order systems.
>
> ✅ **ZOH assumption is mandatory** for the standard discretization formulas above.
>
> ✅ **Stability criterion:** All poles of $\det(zI - G) = 0$ must satisfy $\vert z \vert < 1$.
>
> ✅ **Pole mapping:** $z_i = e^{s_i T}$ — left-half s-plane maps to interior of unit circle.
>
> ✅ **Always assume zero initial conditions** when deriving transfer functions.
>
> ✅ **D matrix** is unchanged by discretization (same in continuous and discrete models).
>
> ✅ **Order of system:** The order of $G_p(z)$ equals the dimension of $G$, i.e., the number of states.
>
> ✅ **Numerical computations:** Always retain at least 4 decimal places for partial credit on approximations.
>
> ✅ **For MIMO systems:** The transfer function becomes a **matrix** $G_p(z) = C(zI-G)^{-1}H + D$ with input-output dimensions preserved.

---
